Medieval improvements: in 1329 the {{c1::butcher's guild of Winchester}} was set up to check meat quality. In 1415 the Mayor of London ordered {{c2::public latrines}} to be rebuilt. In 1385 a {{c3::warden}} was hired to check the Thames was clean. These were {{c4::local}} not national responses.
How did living conditions change (or not) in the Early Modern period (1500–1750)?

Some improvement: stone houses began replacing thatch for the wealthy; piped water arrived for the rich (1600s); flushing closet invented 1596. But for the poor: water sellers still common (now using barrels); cesspits still overflowed; main diet still pottage. Little changed for the majority.

In the Early Modern period, {{c1::Henry VIII}} was the first monarch to issue national plague orders, requiring {{c2::40 days' isolation}} (1518). In 1578, {{c3::Elizabeth I}} introduced {{c4::17 Plague Orders}}, including burning victims' clothing, killing stray animals, and holding funerals after dusk. In 1604, James I added {{c5::hanging and whipping}} as punishments for ignoring plague orders.
What was the Gin Craze and why was it a public health problem?

In the early 1700s, cheap gin flooded London. By the 1720s: crime increased, families fell into poverty, death rates rose (alcohol-related), babies were harmed by mothers drinking while pregnant. Hundreds of thousands drank gin as an escape from hardship.

The {{c1::1729, 1736 and 1743}} Gin Acts introduced taxes and licences for gin sellers but were {{c2::largely ineffective}} — the government did not enforce them and illegal gin shops flourished. The {{c3::1751}} Gin Act was much tougher: only people who paid rent and taxes could sell gin; illegal sellers faced {{c4::imprisonment and whipping}}, and gin drinking was hugely reduced.
What were the key features of Industrial-era living conditions (1750–1900)?

Housing: back-to-back houses; up to 8 people per room; lodging houses; cellars. Water: shared pumps serving up to 200 people; water drawn from polluted canals. Waste: shared privies over cesspools; 'night soil men' collected waste. Food: widespread adulteration (sawdust in bread, chalk in milk, copper in butter).

Cholera first arrived in Britain in {{c1::1831–32}}, killing {{c2::32,000}} people. A second epidemic in {{c3::1877}} killed {{c4::12,000}}. Symptoms were {{c5::fever, diarrhoea and vomiting}}. Most believed it was spread by {{c6::miasma}} (bad air).

In 1842, {{c1::Edwin Chadwick}} published a report linking {{c2::poverty and dirt}} to disease. This led to the {{c3::first Public Health Act in 1848}}, which set up a Central Board of Health. Chadwick argued that {{c4::clean water and sewers}} would reduce disease and poverty.

In 1854, {{c1::John Snow}} investigated a cholera outbreak in {{c2::Soho, London}}. He mapped deaths and found they centred around the {{c3::Broad Street water pump}}. He persuaded the council to {{c4::remove the pump handle}}, ending the outbreak. He proved cholera was spread through {{c5::contaminated water}}, not miasma.

The {{c1::Great Stink}} of {{c2::1858}} made the smell from the River Thames so unbearable that Parliament could not sit. This forced the government to fund {{c3::Joseph Bazalgette}}'s sewer system — over {{c4::1,300 miles}} of sewers were built, cleaning up the Thames and reducing cholera.

The {{c1::1875 Public Health Act}} (under Disraeli) made it {{c2::compulsory}} for local authorities to provide clean water, sewers, and housing inspections. Unlike the {{c3::1848}} Act, which was voluntary, the 1875 Act had real {{c4::enforcement powers}}.

{{c1::Louis Pasteur}} developed germ theory in {{c2::1861}}, proving that {{c3::germs (microbes)}} — not miasma or bad air — caused disease. This was a turning point: governments could now target the {{c4::actual cause}} of disease rather than its symptoms.

The Spanish Flu of {{c1::1918–19}} killed over {{c2::50 million}} people worldwide and over {{c3::228,000}} in the UK. Symptoms began as a common cold then became {{c4::pneumonia}}. The national government response was {{c5::limited}} — key action came from individuals like {{c6::Dr Niven}} in Manchester.

Dr {{c1::Niven}} in Manchester: published detailed advice in {{c2::newspapers and cinemas}}; kept {{c3::detailed case records}} to track patterns; urged councils to {{c4::shut schools and theatres}}; praised NHS workers. Only {{c5::2,000}} died in Manchester — far fewer than London or Glasgow.
What was the 'Homes for Heroes' initiative and who introduced it?

After WW1, Lloyd George's government promised to build council houses ('Homes for Heroes') for returning soldiers. Back-to-back housing was banned in 1909 and removed by 1919. However, Margaret Thatcher sold off council houses in the 1980s — they passed to private landlords who often failed to maintain hygiene standards.

Five phases of UK response to AIDS: 1. Growing {{c1::awareness}} (1970s–1983): first deaths reported, Terrence Higgins Trust set up. 2. Growing {{c2::alarm}} (1984–85): widespread fear and stigma. 3. Growing {{c3::understanding}} (1986–87): clean needle programmes, Princess Diana shook hands with AIDS patients. 4. Growing {{c4::acceptance}} (1988–95): Freddie Mercury concert raised £20 million for research. 5. Growing {{c5::complacency}} (1996+): anti-retroviral drugs led many to believe AIDS was curable.

In {{c1::1987}}, {{c2::Princess Diana}} visited an AIDS clinic and shook hands with a patient, proving AIDS was {{c3::not spread by touch}}. This broke the social stigma and created widespread publicity. In {{c4::1992}}, a tribute concert for Freddie Mercury raised {{c5::£20 million}} for AIDS research.

In {{c1::1996}}, scientists developed {{c2::anti-retroviral}} drugs that slowed the development of AIDS in HIV-positive patients. As a result, the government {{c3::relaxed its campaigns}}, leading to {{c4::growing complacency}}. By 2009, over {{c5::100,000}} people in Britain had HIV.
What does RARE stand for in the context of Nazi Germany's impact on the German people? (Also used in People's Health for 20th century analysis)
Rationing, Area bombing, Refugees, Employment. A framework for analysing the impact of Total War (WW2) on Germany — or used by teachers to structure analysis of modern public health impacts.
How did monasteries contribute to medieval public health?

Infirmaries cared for the sick (Benedictine rule: 'care for the sick stands before all'). Herb/physick gardens grew medicinal plants. Monasteries built near running fresh water — provided drinking and washing water for the sick. Latrines built so waste discharged into rivers. Even without running water, cathedrals built pipes to supply fresh water. Monks believed caring for the sick brought them closer to God. Limited: only helped those who could reach a monastery; no national system.

Leeds local board of health ({{c1::1831}}): set up in response to cholera. Employed surgeon {{c2::Dr Robert Baker}} to investigate. He {{c3::mapped}} the outbreak and found cases concentrated in {{c4::dirty areas}}. Baker believed in {{c5::miasma}} (incorrect), but his report was important evidence linking {{c6::dirt and disease}}. The board also published advice on {{c7::posters and newspapers}} and opened a {{c8::cholera hospital}}.
How can you classify the different responses to cholera in the 1830s?

Three belief categories: (1) Punishment from God — prayer, church attendance, fasting. (2) Miasma — cleaning streets, burning barrels of tar, adding chloride of lime to sewers to mask smells. (3) Contagion — quarantine (stopping people moving from town centres to suburbs), cholera hospitals to isolate victims. Most responses were based on miasma theory — incorrect, but accidentally useful (cleaning streets did reduce disease). John Snow's 1854 waterborne proof was not widely accepted for years.

{{c1::Laissez-faire}}: the political belief that the government should {{c2::not interfere}} in people's everyday lives. This was the main reason the 1848 Public Health Act was {{c3::voluntary}} — the government did not feel it had the right to force local councils to act. It also explains why the {{c4::1751 Gin Act}} was tougher than earlier acts — eventually public opinion shifted enough to force action. Laissez-faire slowed health reform throughout the {{c5::Industrial period}}.
How do you structure the People's Health 18-mark essay (individuals vs government)?

Typical question: 'The actions of great individuals were the most important factor in improving Public Health.' Structure: Para 1 (agree — individual, e.g. John Snow). Para 2 (agree — individual, e.g. Bazalgette/Chadwick). Para 3 (disagree — government, e.g. 1875 Public Health Act compulsory). Para 4 (disagree — events forcing change, e.g. Great Stink, cholera epidemics). Conclusion: both individuals AND government were needed — individuals provided the evidence; government provided the enforcement. Link to laissez-faire as the brake on progress.
What are the key second-order concepts used in OCR History People's Health essays?

Cause, Consequence, Change, Continuity, Significance, Diversity (Typicality). Always name which concept you are using — e.g. 'this shows continuity because...' or 'a consequence of this was...'. The 9-mark summary question requires at least two different second-order concepts.
What key events between 1918 and 1933 helped Hitler rise to power?

1918: Armistice — 'Stab in the Back' myth. 1919: Treaty of Versailles — punishments humiliated Germany. 1919: Hitler joins DAP (German Workers' Party). 1920: 25 Point Programme. 1923: Hyperinflation crisis. 1923: Munich Putsch — failed coup, Hitler jailed. 1925: Mein Kampf published. 1929: Wall Street Crash → Great Depression → 6 million unemployed. January 1933: Hitler appointed Chancellor.

Hitler became {{c1::Chancellor}} of Germany in {{c2::January 1933}}. The Reichstag (parliament building) was set on fire on {{c3::27 February 1933}} — the Nazis blamed the {{c4::Communists}}. This was used to justify banning communists and calling new elections on {{c5::5 March 1933}}.

The {{c1::Enabling Act}} was passed on {{c2::24 March 1933}}. It gave Hitler the power to {{c3::pass laws without consulting the Reichstag}} for four years. This made him a dictator. Trade unions were banned on {{c4::2 May 1933}}. All political parties were banned in {{c5::July 1933}}.

The Night of the Long Knives took place on {{c1::29–30 June 1934}}. Hitler had the leaders of the {{c2::SA (Sturmabteilung)}} killed, including {{c3::Ernst Röhm}}. This removed a rival power base and reassured the {{c4::army}}. After this, Hitler was seen as above the law.

President Hindenburg died on {{c1::2 August 1934}}. Hitler immediately combined the roles of {{c2::Chancellor and President}}, calling himself {{c3::der Führer}} (the Leader). The army swore an oath of personal loyalty {{c4::to Hitler}} (not Germany) on {{c5::August 1934}}.
How did the Nazis control Germany through fear?

SS (Schutzstaffel): Hitler's personal bodyguard and elite force — ran concentration camps. SD (Sicherheitsdienst): Nazi intelligence service. Gestapo: secret state police — used informers and torture; 26% of cases came from public denunciations. People's Court: dealt with political crimes; unfair trials. Concentration camps: first opened March 1933; held political opponents.

The Gestapo had very few officers — they relied on the public to {{c1::inform on their neighbours}}. In Düsseldorf, {{c2::26%}} of cases came from the {{c3::general public}}. Despite Nazi propaganda suggesting agents were {{c4::everywhere}}, the reality was that Germans {{c5::informed on each other}} voluntarily.
How did the Nazis control Germany through propaganda?

Goebbels ran the Ministry of Propaganda. Controlled: all radio stations (taken over 1934), newspapers, cinema, art, music, theatre, school curriculum. Rallies like Nuremberg used spectacle and emotion. Public loudspeakers broadcast Hitler's speeches in workplaces, shops and pubs. Key message: national pride, anti-Semitism, racial purity.
Who opposed the Nazis between 1933 and 1939, and how effective were they?

Social Democrats and Communists: forced underground; had limited impact. Christians for/against: Concordat 1933 initially silenced Catholics; 1937 Papal Encyclical 'With Burning Anxiety' opposed the Nazis. Swing Kids: rejected Nazi music for jazz; minor youth rebellion. Edelweiss Pirates: youth groups who fought Hitler Youth and sang anti-Nazi songs — not organised enough to threaten the regime.
What did the Nazis want from German workers, and what was the reality?

Wanted: full employment, loyalty, productivity. Did: Strength Through Joy (cheap holidays, leisure), Beauty of Labour (improve workplaces), abolish trade unions. Results: unemployment fell from 6 million (1933) to near zero (1938). BUT: workers had no rights, could not strike, wages were controlled. Standard of living for workers was mixed.

Nazi policies towards women: marriage loans of up to {{c1::1,000 Reichsmarks}} offered to Aryan couples — reduced by {{c2::a quarter}} for each child born. The {{c3::Honour Cross of the German Mother}} was awarded: {{c4::bronze}} for 4–5 children, {{c5::silver}} for 6–7, {{c6::gold}} for 8+.

Nazi women policies: the number of marriages {{c1::increased}} from 516,000 (1932) to 772,000 (1939). But women in employment {{c2::also increased}} — in agriculture from 4.6 million to {{c3::4.9 million}}; in industry from 2.7 to {{c4::3.3 million}}. Female university enrolment was capped at {{c5::10%}}. The Nazis' aims for women were {{c6::largely unsuccessful}}.
How did the Nazis control young people through education and youth groups?

Schools: all teachers had to join the National Socialist Teachers League (1933); curriculum pushed Nazi ideology in every subject (e.g. maths problems about racial 'impurity'). Youth groups: Hitler Youth (boys 10–18) and League of German Maidens (BDM, girls) — cheap holidays, hikes, sports, Nazi songs. Membership was compulsory from 1936. Some children informed on parents to the Gestapo.

The {{c1::Nuremberg Laws}} were passed in {{c2::1935}}. They stripped German Jews of {{c3::citizenship}} and banned marriage or sexual relations between Jews and {{c4::Aryans}}.{{c5::Kristallnacht}} (Night of Broken Glass) occurred on {{c6::9–10 November 1938}}: Jewish businesses, synagogues and homes were attacked; around {{c7::100}} Jews were killed and {{c8::30,000}} arrested.
What were the stages of Nazi persecution of Jews leading to the Final Solution?

1933–38: boycotts, civil service sackings, Nuremberg Laws. 1938: Kristallnacht. First solution (1938–39): forced emigration. Second solution (1939–41): concentration in ghettos (e.g. Warsaw). 1941: Einsatzgruppen (mobile killing units) followed the army into the Soviet Union. 1942: Wannsee Conference planned the Final Solution. Death camps set up in Eastern Europe (e.g. Auschwitz, Treblinka).
How did World War II affect Germany in its early stages (1939–42)?

Initially positive: Germany gained food, raw materials from conquered territory. 2/5 Germans actually ate better due to rationing. Luxury goods imported (mostly to senior Nazis). But: coal shortages in winter 1940; rationing queues; children evacuated; up to 4 air raids a week.

{{c1::Albert Speer}} was put in charge of the German war economy in {{c2::1942}}. He reorganised production and increased output. {{c3::Total War}} was declared in {{c4::February 1943}} after the German defeat at {{c5::Stalingrad}}, requiring all resources to be focused on the war effort.

Use the RARE framework to analyse the impact of WW2 on Germans: {{c1::R}}ationing (diets became bread, potato, preserves by 1945), {{c2::A}}rea bombing (500,000 Berliners homeless by Nov 1943; 400,000 Germans killed in raids 1943–45), {{c3::R}}efugees (11 million ethnic Germans expelled by 1945), {{c4::E}}mployment (7.6 million forced foreign workers; elderly and Hitler Youth conscripted 1944).
Who opposed the Nazis during World War II and how effective were they?

White Rose (Munich University, 1942–43): Hans and Sophie Scholl distributed anti-Nazi leaflets; arrested and executed February 1943. July Bomb Plot (1944): senior military officers tried to assassinate Hitler; failed; 5,000 executed. Rosenstrasse (February 1943): German women protested for release of Jewish husbands; succeeded. Cardinal Galen (1941): publicly condemned Nazi euthanasia programme. Passive resistance: absenteeism, spreading rumours, listening to BBC.
How did the Nazis treat occupied Poland and the Netherlands differently?

Poland: first country occupied (1939); 500,000 Germans moved in, Poles moved out; Hans Frank ruled by terror; 1.9 million Poles killed; 3 of 3.5 million Polish Jews murdered; extermination camps built. Netherlands (occupied May 1940): Dutch seen as Aryan, so no mass murder; no German resettlement; but Dutch Jews still deported 1943; 500,000 Dutch workers sent to Germany as forced labour.
What were the OCR Nazi Germany exam question types and mark allocations?

7-mark source question: analyse usefulness of one source (content, origin, purpose). 15-mark question: analyse two sources and one interpretation. 18-mark essay: 4 paragraphs + one-sentence intro + conclusion. Each paragraph starts 'I agree/disagree because…'. Always include specific facts and link back to the question.

Key Nazi Germany chronology: 1918 {{c1::Armistice}}; 1919 {{c2::Treaty of Versailles}}; 1923 {{c3::Munich Putsch}}; Jan 1933 {{c4::Chancellor}}; Feb 1933 {{c5::Reichstag Fire}}; March 1933 {{c6::Enabling Act}}; June 1934 {{c7::Night of the Long Knives}}; Aug 1934 {{c8::Hindenburg dies → Führer}}; 1935 {{c9::Nuremberg Laws}}; Nov 1938 {{c10::Kristallnacht}}; Sept 1939 {{c11::invasion of Poland}}; 1942 {{c12::Wannsee Conference}}; July 1944 {{c13::Bomb Plot}}; April 1945 {{c14::Hitler's suicide}}.
How do you classify Hitler's steps to dictatorship as legal or violent?

Legal steps: Reichstag Fire (used legally to ban Communists and call elections); Enabling Act (passed by the Reichstag — legal but forced); ban on all other political parties July 1933; Hindenburg's death merged roles legally. Violent steps: SA intimidation during 1933 elections; Night of the Long Knives (political murder); SS/Gestapo used terror. Key argument: Hitler used a mixture of both — the legal veneer made it harder for opponents to resist, and harder for foreign powers to intervene.
How do you answer the Nazi Germany 7-mark source question (Content, Origin, Purpose)?

Structure: (1) Content — what does the source actually say/show? Quote it directly and explain what it tells us. (2) Origin — who made it, when, what type of source? Consider what this means for its reliability. (3) Purpose — why was it made? What was it trying to do? (e.g. to persuade, to inform, to justify). Example: Source A (German newspaper 1934 ordering loudspeakers for Hitler's speech) → Content: shows propaganda reach into workplaces. Origin: official Nazi newspaper, 1934. Purpose: to maximise exposure to Hitler's message and create impression of total support.
How do you answer the Nazi Germany 15-mark question (two sources + interpretation)?

Structure: Three paragraphs — each like a mini 7-mark answer. Para 1: What does Source A suggest? Use content/origin/purpose. Para 2: What does Source B suggest? Same structure. Para 3: What does the interpretation suggest? How does it compare to the sources — does it agree or disagree? Always use specific quotes from the sources. The sources and interpretation are usually on the same theme — identify what they agree/disagree on.

Example 7-mark source: Source A is a {{c1::German newspaper announcement (March 1934)}} ordering that loudspeakers be placed in {{c2::factories, shops, pubs and blocks of flats}} so people could hear Hitler's speech. Content shows: propaganda was spread into {{c3::all areas of everyday life}}. Origin: an official {{c4::Nazi}} source, so likely exaggerates compliance. Purpose: to {{c5::maximise the audience}} for Hitler's speeches and reinforce the impression of {{c6::total national unity}}.
What is the structure of Making of America and what period does it cover?

The Making of America, 1789–1900. Covers: American expansion 1789–1838 (Constitution, slavery, Indian removal); The West 1839–60 (Plains Indians, migration, gold rush); Civil War and Reconstruction 1861–77; Settlement and conflict on the Plains 1861–77; American cultures 1877–1900 (changing lives of Plains Indians and Black Americans).

The {{c1::Constitution}} set out the rules for how the USA would be governed. The country was divided into {{c2::states}} (fully governed) and {{c3::territories}} (not yet states). The {{c4::Louisiana Purchase}} in {{c5::1803}} — President Jefferson bought the Louisiana colony from France for {{c6::$15 million}}, doubling the size of the USA.

In 1793, {{c1::Eli Whitney}} invented the {{c2::cotton gin}}, which could process cotton {{c3::50 times faster}} than by hand. This led to a massive expansion of cotton production in the South and a huge increase in demand for {{c4::enslaved people}}.

In 1820, the {{c1::Missouri Compromise}} agreed that slavery would not be allowed {{c2::north and west of Missouri}}. This temporarily resolved tensions between North and South. Only {{c3::25%}} of Southerners owned slaves; by 1820, cotton made up {{c4::42%}} of all US exports.
What were conditions like for enslaved people in the Cotton Kingdom?

The 'pushing system' forced enslaved people to farm more land each year (5 acres in 1805; 10 acres in 1850). Families were separated at slave auctions. A healthy 18-year-old male could sell for $900–$1,100 in New Orleans. Between 1815 and 1820, 2,646 children under 13 were sold in New Orleans alone. Women were searched naked to check fertility.

New Orleans was the centre of the {{c1::Cotton Kingdom}}. It {{c2::tripled}} in size between 1803 and 1819, becoming the {{c3::4th largest}} city in the USA. Land {{c4::speculators}} (often from the North) bought land in new territories hoping to make profits by running {{c5::plantations}}.

The {{c1::Indian Removal Act}} was passed by President {{c2::Andrew Jackson}} in {{c3::1830}}. It moved the {{c4::Five Civilised Tribes}} (Cherokee, Creek, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Seminole) from the south-east to {{c5::Indian Territory}} west of the Mississippi.

The {{c1::Trail of Tears}} (1838): the US army forced {{c2::18,000}} Cherokee into concentration camps, then marched them to Indian Territory. Over {{c3::4,000}} died. The {{c4::Seminole}} refused to leave and fought a costly war — the US spent {{c5::$40–60 million}}; the army killed Seminole chief {{c6::Osceola}} in 1842.
What was the culture of the Plains Indians (focus on Lakota Sioux)?

Nomadic lifestyle — followed the buffalo for food, clothing, shelter (tepees). Leadership by councils of elders and chiefs; decisions made by consensus. Buffalo sacred — used for everything (food, clothing, tools, fuel). Believed land could not be owned — it belonged to everyone. Warfare was about honour (counting coup), not conquest. Clashed fundamentally with white American values.

{{c1::Manifest Destiny}} was the belief that it was America's God-given duty to expand westward across the continent. This justified white settlement on Indian lands. The {{c2::Pre-emption Act of 1841}} gave farmers who cleared land and built a house in Oregon the {{c3::first right to buy}} it.

The Mormons settled in {{c1::Salt Lake City, Utah}} in {{c2::1847}} after their leader {{c3::Brigham Young}} had a vision. They succeeded because: Young's leadership was {{c4::unquestioned}} (seen as God's link); land was {{c5::centrally divided by the Church}} (not privately owned); they built {{c6::irrigation ditches}} immediately to allow farming.

Gold was discovered in {{c1::California}} in {{c2::1848}}. The 'Gold Rush' brought {{c3::thousands}} of miners West in 1849. This led to conflict with Plains Indians as migration routes crossed their lands. The Fort Laramie Treaty of {{c4::1851}} gave Plains tribes compensation for damage to hunting grounds in return for allowing safe routes through.
What were the causes of the American Civil War (1861–65)?

North–South economic/cultural divide (industrial North vs agricultural, slave South). Abolitionist pressure (Garrison, Douglass, Tubman, Beecher-Stowe). Missouri Compromise 1820 — uneasy truce. Clay Compromise 1850 — free states must return escaped slaves. Kansas-Nebraska Act 1854 — popular sovereignty on slavery → 'Bleeding Kansas'. Republican Party founded as anti-slavery party. Lincoln elected president 1860 — South Carolina seceded immediately. Fort Sumter attacked 12 April 1861 → war declared.

The {{c1::Kansas-Nebraska Act 1854}} overturned the Missouri Compromise and allowed territories to vote on slavery ('{{c2::popular sovereignty}}'). This led to violent clashes known as '{{c3::Bleeding Kansas}}'. It led to the founding of the {{c4::Republican Party}} as an anti-slavery party.

{{c1::Abraham Lincoln}} was elected President in {{c2::November 1860}}. {{c3::South Carolina}} seceded immediately; six more states followed. They formed the {{c4::Confederacy}}. On {{c5::12 April 1861}}, Confederate forces fired on {{c6::Fort Sumter}}; Lincoln declared war three days later.

Key abolitionists: {{c1::William Lloyd Garrison}} created the newspaper 'The Liberator' (1830) and the National Anti-Slavery Society (1833). {{c2::Frederick Douglass}} was a freed slave who lectured about slavery's horrors. {{c3::Harriet Tubman}} helped thousands escape via the Underground Railroad. {{c4::Harriet Beecher-Stowe}} published 'Uncle Tom's Cabin' in 1851, selling over {{c5::2 million}} copies.
What was the experience of Black Americans during the Civil War (1861–65)?

Before 1861: in North, free but few rights; in South, enslaved. 1862: some escaped slaves used as labourers by Union army. 1863: Emancipation Proclamation freed slaves in Confederate states. Black regiments formed but initially: no equal pay; no officer roles; race riots in Northern cities. By war's end: 180,000 Black soldiers served in the Union army; gained citizenship rights in principle.

The {{c1::Emancipation Proclamation}} (January 1863) freed enslaved people in the {{c2::Confederate states}}. The {{c3::13th Amendment}} (1865) abolished slavery throughout the USA. The {{c4::14th Amendment}} (1868) granted citizenship to all people born in the USA. The {{c5::15th Amendment}} (1870) gave Black men the right to vote.
What happened during Reconstruction (1865–77) and how limited was the progress?

Freedmen's Bureau set up to help formerly enslaved people. Land given to Black families in Sea Islands, South Carolina. Missionaries taught reading and writing. BUT: sharecropping replaced slavery economically — freed people farmed land in exchange for a share of the crop (remained in poverty). KKK used terror to stop Black people voting. By 1877, federal troops withdrew and Southern states rolled back rights.

Little Crow's War (1862): the Santee Sioux were {{c1::starving}} on their reservation in Minnesota. When Little Crow asked for food, a white trader said '{{c2::let them eat grass}}'. The Sioux attacked whites; the trader was found dead with his {{c3::mouth stuffed with grass}}. {{c4::38}} Santee Sioux were hanged before a cheering crowd.

Sand Creek Massacre ({{c1::1864}}): US soldiers attacked a peaceful Cheyenne and Arapaho camp led by {{c2::Black Kettle}}, killing around {{c3::150–200}} people including women and children. Key word: {{c4::guns}} — the US army used superior firepower against a peaceful group.

Red Cloud's War (1866–68): the Lakota Sioux, led by {{c1::Red Cloud}}, fought to close the {{c2::Bozeman Trail}} through their hunting lands. The US army was forced to sign the {{c3::Fort Laramie Treaty 1868}}, abandoning the trail. Key word: {{c4::gold}} — miners used the trail to reach Montana gold fields.

The Great Sioux War (1876–77): Lieutenant Colonel {{c1::Custer}} and 260 soldiers were defeated at the {{c2::Battle of Little Bighorn}} (1876) by Sioux led by {{c3::Sitting Bull}} and Crazy Horse. Key word: {{c4::Sitting Bull's vision}} — he saw soldiers falling like grasshoppers. Despite this victory, the Sioux were eventually forced onto reservations.

The {{c1::Dawes Act 1887}} broke up Indian reservations into individual plots of land. Indians who farmed their plots and 'behaved' could become US citizens. It destroyed {{c2::tribal culture}} and benefitted {{c3::white land speculators}}, who bought 'surplus' reservation land. {{c4::Carlisle Indian School}} was one of many boarding schools that forcibly assimilated Indian children, banning their language and culture.
What happened to Black Americans in the period 1877–1900?

Reconstruction ended 1877 — federal troops withdrew; Southern states introduced Jim Crow laws (segregation). Sharecropping kept most Black Southerners in poverty. Lynching increased — KKK used terror. Exodusters: around 40,000 Black Americans moved to Kansas in 1879, escaping Southern violence. Booker T. Washington argued for gradual progress through education and work.

A {{c1::coffle}} was a chained group of slaves force-marched for months from old states (Maryland, North Carolina) to the Deep South. When they arrived, slaves were {{c2::deloused and fed butter}} to regain weight. Once restored, a slave could sell for over {{c3::$1,000}}. So much money was made that slave traders even began to {{c4::kidnap free Black people in the Northern states}} to sell into slavery.
What was Rachel's story and why is it significant?

Rachel arrived in New Orleans in 1819. Women were searched naked to check if they could have children. In January 1819 a healthy 18-year-old male sold for $900–$1,100. Rachel was sold for $800. Her story is significant because it shows the dehumanisation of enslaved people — reduced to their physical capacity to work and reproduce — and evidence that women were sold at lower prices than men in this market.
What are the four key Native American conflicts and their key words?

Grass — Little Crow's War (1862): Santee Sioux starving; trader said 'let them eat grass'; trader found dead with mouth stuffed with grass. Guns — Sand Creek Massacre (1864): US army used superior firepower to massacre peaceful Cheyenne. Gold — Red Cloud's War (1866–68): miners used Bozeman Trail to reach Montana gold fields; Sioux forced US to close it. Sitting Bull's Vision — Great Sioux War (1876): Sitting Bull saw soldiers falling like grasshoppers before Battle of Little Bighorn.
What are the OCR Making of America exam question types and mark allocations?

1-mark questions (×3): give a specific fact — very short answers. 9-mark summary: analyse a topic with examples; use two different second-order concepts. 10-mark 'why/impact' question: 3–4 bullet-point paragraphs + one-sentence conclusion; always answer WHY, not just narrate events. 18-mark essay: 4 paragraphs + introduction + conclusion; argue a clear agree/disagree.
Who were the Bigods and why did they choose Framlingham?

Roger Bigod I was granted the manor in 1101 by Henry I. Framlingham was already a reasonably sized town (600 people, worth £44 in the Domesday Book). It had a Saxon settlement and was a strategic location. Roger likely built a timber fortress overlooking the mere. The Bigods became Earls of Norfolk — one of the most powerful noble families in England.

Hugh Bigod (1st Earl of Norfolk) made changes to his father's timber fortress: a {{c1::stone chamber block}} with {{c2::2}} storeys, {{c3::fireplaces and stone chimneys}} (2 survive today), a large {{c4::timber hall}} for feasting, and a {{c5::chapel}}. The {{c6::chapel window}} in the curtain wall still survives. Hugh later sided with {{c7::Empress Matilda}} against King Stephen, so Henry II had the castle destroyed in {{c8::1175}}.

Roger Bigod II rebuilt Framlingham from {{c1::1189}} under King Richard I. He built the massive {{c2::stone curtain wall}} we see today. In {{c3::1213}} he hosted King John at the castle. He was among the barons who forced King John to sign the {{c4::Magna Carta}} in {{c5::1215}}. As punishment, King John {{c6::besieged}} Framlingham and seized it in {{c7::1216}}.
What are the key physical features of the Bigod curtain wall at Framlingham?

Built c.1190. Stands 10.5m high and 2.3m thick. Built from local flint and septaria stone. 13 towers rising 3.8m above the wall walk — backless (no enclosed interior). Arrow loops for archers. Holes for wooden shutters. Crenellations at the top. Wall walk wide enough for two people to pass.
What are the key defensive features Roger Bigod II added to Framlingham?

Two deep dry ditches around the castle (not water-filled) — to prevent tunnelling and make the walls look more intimidating. Gatehouse with drawbridge and portcullis (portcullis slot still visible; actual portcullis gone). Well 30m deep near the gate — essential for withstanding a siege. All visible from the castle today.

Framlingham's {{c1::well}} is {{c2::30m}} deep and located near the gate. It was essential in times of {{c3::siege}} for fresh water and sanitation. The {{c4::gatehouse}} still shows the {{c5::portcullis slot}} above the gate-tower, but the portcullis itself is {{c6::gone}}. The latrines expelled waste down chutes through the curtain wall — an example survives on {{c7::Tower 13}}.

The Great Hall was built by {{c1::Roger Bigod II}} for dining and entertaining, including {{c2::King John}} in 1213. It was located where the {{c3::poorhouse}} building now stands and would have been open to the roof with an open {{c4::hearth}}. Today only {{c5::wooden beam holes}} in the curtain wall survive to show where it stood.
How did the Howard family come to own Framlingham and what changes did they make?

John Howard was made Duke of Norfolk in 1483 by Richard III. After the Battle of Bosworth (1485) the castle briefly passed to John de Vere. Thomas Howard I recovered it from 1489, becoming a leading Tudor courtier. The Howards added: decorative brick chimneys (both functional and fake, imitating Hampton Court style), wider gatehouse, red brick buildings, a bridge to pleasure gardens. Thomas Howard I died at Framlingham aged 80 in 1524.

The Howard chimneys at Framlingham were built to imitate the style of {{c1::Hampton Court}}, Henry VIII's palace. Some chimneys are {{c2::functional}} (attached to fireplaces) and some are {{c3::fake}} (built purely for show). This shows the Howards were keen to display {{c4::wealth and power}} and their links to {{c5::Henry VIII}}. Thomas Howard promoted two nieces as Henry's wives: {{c6::Anne Boleyn}} (2nd wife) and {{c7::Catherine Howard}} (5th wife) — both were beheaded.

In {{c1::1552}}, Framlingham was given to {{c2::Mary Tudor}} in Henry VIII's will. When Edward VI died in {{c3::1553}}, he tried to make {{c4::Lady Jane Grey}} queen. Mary fled to Framlingham and on {{c5::12 July 1553}} raised her standard and rallied her troops. By {{c6::19 July}} the Privy Council accepted her as queen. She was the first {{c7::Queen Regnant}} of England.
How did Framlingham change under Elizabeth I (1558–1603)?

The fourth Howard duke (Elizabeth's relative through Anne Boleyn) was initially the premier nobleman. But he was executed in 1572 for plotting to marry Mary Queen of Scots. Framlingham passed back to the Crown. By 1580 the Great Park was turned into fields. From 1580 the castle was used as a prison — by 1600 it held 40 Roman Catholic priests and recusants. A 1589 survey estimated £100 of urgent repairs were needed.

Sir {{c1::Robert Hitcham}} bought Framlingham in {{c2::1635}} for £{{c3::14,000}}. He came from humble origins but became a lawyer to {{c4::James I}} and an MP for Suffolk. In his will he instructed that 'all the castle, saving the {{c5::stone walls}}, be pulled down' and a {{c6::poorhouse}} built. Pembroke College, {{c7::Cambridge}} was left the castle on the condition they carried out his wishes.
What happened to the poorhouse at Framlingham (1635–1839)?

Hitcham died 1636; legal wrangling delayed action. Almshouses built 1654; Red House erected soon after. 1666: plague struck Framlingham; castle buildings used as isolation wards. Funds mismanaged; poorhouse closed temporarily. 1699: failed attempt to re-establish poorhouse for children. 1729: new poorhouse built on site of medieval Great Hall with parish funds. Housed adults and children until 1839, when poor were moved to Wickham Market workhouse. Building became a parish hall — now a tourist attraction run by English Heritage.
How is Framlingham typical or atypical compared to other castles?

Typical: curtain wall of local flint and septaria stone similar to Orford Castle (12 miles away, also built c.1166–73); both built as baronial power bases; both had ditches and gatehouses. Atypical: Framlingham has no keep (shell keep or stone tower) — unusual for a Norman castle; it has 13 backless towers (no enclosed rooms). Unlike many castles, Framlingham never fell out of use — it served as castle, prison, and poorhouse continuously.
What are the key exam question themes for the OCR Historical Environment paper (Framlingham)?

(a) Reasons for location. (b) When/why first created. (c) Ways the site has changed over time. (d) How the site has been used throughout its history. (e) Diversity of activities and people. (f) Reasons for changes. (g) Significant times: peak activity, turning points. (h) Significance of specific physical features. (i) Local or national importance. (j) Typicality vs other sites. (k) What the site reveals about attitudes and values in particular periods.
What are the main periods and owners of Framlingham Castle in chronological order?

1101–1216: Bigod family (Roger I, Hugh, Roger II). 1216: King John seizes it after Magna Carta siege. 1483–1547: Howard family (John, Thomas I, Thomas II). 1547: Crown (Edward VI). 1552–58: Mary Tudor. 1558–72: Fourth Howard duke, then Crown again. 1580s–1603: Prison under Elizabeth I. 1635: sold to Robert Hitcham. 1636 onwards: poorhouse under Pembroke College, Cambridge. 1839: poorhouse closes. Now: English Heritage tourist site.

In {{c1::1177}}, King Henry II ordered the demolition of Framlingham Castle because {{c2::Hugh Bigod}} had rebelled. A group of carpenters and masons led by {{c3::Alnoth the Engineer}} carried out the work. The cost recorded in the Pipe Rolls was {{c4::£7 10s 6d}}. The same Alnoth then built {{c5::Orford Castle}} for Henry II — to keep an eye on the Bigods.
What did each Howard individually contribute to Framlingham?

John Howard (1st Duke, 1483): made Duke of Norfolk by Richard III; added red brick buildings; died at Battle of Bosworth 1485 fighting for Richard III. Thomas Howard I (2nd Duke): won Battle of Flodden Field 1513 aged 70; died at Framlingham aged 80 in 1524; lavish funeral procession to Thetford Priory attended by 900+ mourners. Thomas Howard II (3rd Duke): promoted nieces Anne Boleyn and Catherine Howard as Henry's wives — both beheaded; imprisoned in Tower of London by Henry VIII; released by Mary I.

Thomas Howard I's funeral (1524): body lay in state in the {{c1::castle chapel}} for {{c2::four weeks}}. A procession of over {{c3::900}} mourners in black went {{c4::30 miles}} to {{c5::Thetford Priory}}. Alms were given to up to {{c6::10,000}} people. This shows Framlingham's {{c7::local}} importance as a centre of noble power.
What barriers did Mary Tudor face when claiming the throne in 1553, and how did Framlingham help?

Barriers: Edward VI died aged 15; the Protestant Duke of Northumberland planned to arrest Mary and install Lady Jane Grey (his daughter-in-law). Mary was Catholic and isolated. Northumberland controlled London. Framlingham's role: Mary fled there. She sent letters to noblemen; many gathered at Framlingham for religious or dynastic reasons. On 12 July she 'raised her standard'. Northumberland halted at Cambridge, intimidated by her forces. By 19 July the Privy Council accepted her — she was proclaimed Queen at Framlingham. Lady Jane Grey's reign lasted just nine days.
What is the Western Tower at Framlingham and what does it reveal?

Also called the Prison Tower — used during Elizabeth I's reign when Framlingham housed 40 Roman Catholic priests and recusants. It survives today as physical evidence of how the castle's use changed from a noble residence to a prison as anti-Catholic laws hardened from 1580.
How typical is Framlingham compared to Orford Castle?

Similarities: both in Suffolk (12 miles apart); both use local flint and septaria stone; both have gatehouses, portcullises, arrow loops, and wells; both built on natural spurs. Key difference: Orford has a single polygonal keep — Framlingham has NO keep (rooms spread around the curtain wall). Orford was built by Henry II specifically to watch the rebellious Bigods. Alnoth the Engineer built Orford immediately after demolishing Framlingham in 1177.
How typical is Framlingham compared to the Tower of London and Norwich Castle?

Tower of London: similar gatehouses, portcullises, arrow loops, wells — but defended by River Thames not a spur; has a single keep (White Tower); built by William the Conqueror to show Norman power. Norwich Castle: originally a motte-and-bailey with a single keep on top of the mound; the Bigod Arch (original entrance) shows decorative stonework; both Norwich and Framlingham were besieged by King John after Magna Carta 1215. Key shared atypicality: Framlingham alone has NO keep.
What was the key turning point in Framlingham's history and how do physical features show it?

The change from a medieval fortress (Bigod era) to an impressive noble home (Howard era) — driven by the fact that by the 16th century, castles were no longer needed for warfare. Physical evidence: the Bigod curtain wall and towers (defence) versus the Howard decorative chimneys (status). The chimneys — both functional and fake, imitating Hampton Court — reveal the Howards' priority was impressing Henry VIII rather than defending against attack.
What OCR exam technique is needed for the Framlingham 20-mark question?

Always name your site and period in the first line. Use specific physical features with precise detail (e.g. '10.5m high', '13 towers', 'portcullis slot'). Explain what the feature reveals — what it tells us about attitudes, priorities, or events. Address challenges and benefits of using the physical remains. Link physical features to wider history (e.g. chimneys = imitating Hampton Court = Howard ambition). End with a conclusion that directly answers the question.
What are the main word classes and why do they matter in analysis?

Noun: a person, place or thing (concrete = physical, abstract = idea/emotion). Verb: an action or state of being. Adjective: describes a noun. Adverb: modifies a verb, adjective or other adverb. Pronoun: replaces a noun (I, we, you). Always name the word class when analysing — e.g. 'the verb X suggests...' 'the abstract noun X connotes...'

{{c1::Concrete noun}}: a physical, tangible thing (e.g. 'stone', 'hands'). {{c2::Abstract noun}}: an idea, emotion or concept (e.g. 'freedom', 'grief'). Abstract nouns are powerful in analysis as they signal {{c3::themes and emotional weight}}.

{{c1::Fronted syntax}}: placing the most important word at the {{c2::beginning}} of a sentence or line. E.g. '{{c3::Suddenly}} he was awake and running' — the adverb 'suddenly' creates {{c4::immediate disorientation}}.

{{c1::Terminal position}}: placing the most important word at the {{c2::end}} of a sentence or line. Creates {{c3::finality, impact or revelation}}. E.g. 'Nothing beside {{c4::remains}}' — 'remains' lands last, emphasising {{c5::emptiness}}.
What are the four sentence types and what effects do they create?

Declarative: states a fact — creates authority and certainty. Interrogative: asks a question — creates uncertainty, engages reader, challenges. Exclamatory: expresses strong emotion — creates urgency or passion. Imperative: gives a command — creates power and directness. Identifying the sentence type is a quick structural analysis point.

A {{c1::declarative}} sentence states a fact. An {{c2::interrogative}} asks a question. An {{c3::exclamatory}} expresses strong emotion. An {{c4::imperative}} gives a command. In analysis, always link the sentence type to its {{c5::effect on the reader}}.

{{c1::Semantic field}}: a group of words all related to the same topic or idea. E.g. a semantic field of {{c2::violence}} in Macbeth ('blood', 'murder', 'execution'). Tracking a semantic field shows a writer's {{c3::preoccupation}} with a theme.

{{c1::Extended metaphor}}: a metaphor that is developed across several lines or throughout a whole text. E.g. the {{c2::plant metaphor}} in Macbeth runs from start to end. Extended metaphors create {{c3::thematic coherence}}.
{{c1::Pathetic fallacy}}: when {{c2::weather or the natural environment}} reflects the emotional state of characters or the mood of a scene. E.g. the storm on the night of Duncan's murder in Macbeth.

{{c1::Dramatic irony}}: when the {{c2::audience/reader}} knows something that the {{c3::character}} does not. E.g. the audience knows Macbeth plans murder when Duncan calls him 'most worthiest cousin'.
{{c1::Foreshadowing}}: when a writer hints at {{c2::events that will happen later}} in the text. Creates a sense of {{c3::inevitability}} and rewards re-reading.

{{c1::Euphemism}}: using mild or indirect language to refer to something {{c2::unpleasant}}. E.g. 'I gave commands' (My Last Duchess) is a euphemism for {{c3::murder}}. Shows the speaker's moral detachment.
{{c1::Hubris}}: excessive {{c2::pride}} that leads to a character's downfall. In Macbeth, Hubris = believing the witches' prophecies make him {{c3::invincible}}.
{{c1::Hamartia}}: a character's {{c2::fatal flaw}} that leads to their downfall (Ancient Greek, from Aristotle). Macbeth's hamartia is {{c3::ambition}}. Used in tragedy.
{{c1::Peripeteia}}: the {{c2::turning point}} or unexpected reversal of circumstances in a tragedy. In Macbeth, the peripeteia is {{c3::the murder of Duncan}} — after this, decline is inevitable.

{{c1::Catharsis}}: the {{c2::emotional release}} felt by the audience at the end of a tragedy. The audience has experienced fear and pity alongside Macbeth — his death provides {{c3::relief and moral clarity}}.

{{c1::Antithesis}}: placing two {{c2::opposite}} ideas in parallel structure to highlight contrast. E.g. '{{c3::Fair is foul, and foul is fair}}' — the antithesis establishes the play's central theme of {{c4::appearance vs reality}}.

{{c1::Soliloquy}}: a speech where a character speaks {{c2::alone on stage}}, revealing their {{c3::inner thoughts}} to the audience. Only used in drama. Gives the audience privileged access to {{c4::true motivation}}.
{{c1::Aside}}: a short remark spoken by a character {{c2::directly to the audience}}, unheard by other characters. Shows a {{c3::gap between public behaviour and private thought}}.
{{c1::Motif}}: a {{c2::recurring image, symbol or idea}} throughout a text that develops thematic meaning. E.g. blood in Macbeth, the conch in LOTF, fog in Jekyll & Hyde.

{{c1::Equivocation}}: using deliberately {{c2::ambiguous language}} to mislead. The witches equivocate: 'none of woman born shall harm Macbeth' is technically true but {{c3::misleading}}. Links to appearance vs reality.
{{c1::Epizeuxis}}: the immediate {{c2::repetition of a word or phrase}} for emphasis. E.g. '{{c3::O horror! Horror! Horror!}}' — the repetition conveys stupefaction beyond words.
What does 'zooming in' mean in language analysis and why does your teacher emphasise it?

Zooming in means selecting ONE specific word (ideally a single verb, noun or adjective) and unpacking its connotations in detail rather than commenting broadly. E.g. instead of 'the language is violent', zoom in: 'the verb unseamed has connotations of surgical precision, suggesting Macbeth's violence is controlled and purposeful rather than chaotic.' The more specific the word class and the richer the connotations, the higher the mark.
What is the difference between connotation and denotation?

Denotation: the literal, dictionary definition of a word. Connotation: the associations, feelings or ideas a word carries beyond its literal meaning. In analysis you always want connotations. E.g. 'red' denotation = a colour. Connotations = danger, blood, passion, warning. Good analysis goes beyond what a word means to what it suggests.
What are the three levels of analysis for any quotation?

1. Identify the technique/word class — name it precisely. 2. Zoom in on the specific word — what does it literally mean? 3. Expand to connotations/effect — what does it suggest? What does it make the reader feel? Link to the writer's intent/context. Never stop at level 1 (identifying). A grade 9 response lives at level 3.

{{c1::Cyclical structure}}: when the {{c2::ending mirrors or echoes the opening}}, creating a sense of completion or irony. E.g. Macbeth opens and closes with battle and a rightful king — the {{c3::natural order is restored}}.

{{c1::Iambic pentameter}}: the 'natural' rhythm of English speech — da-DUM × 5 per line. When characters break from it, something is {{c2::wrong or disordered}}. The witches speak in {{c3::trochaic tetrameter}} (opposite rhythm) — marking them as {{c4::unnatural}}.

{{c1::Rhyming couplet}}: two consecutive lines that rhyme. In Shakespeare, used to {{c2::signal the end of a scene}} or emphasise a character's resolution. E.g. 'Stars, hide your fires / Let not light see my black and deep {{c3::desires}}'.
How do you approach an unseen poem — what three things must you always analyse?

1. Language — zoom in on specific word choices (verbs, nouns, adjectives), name techniques (metaphor, sibilance, plosive etc.), explore connotations. 2. Structure — form (sonnet, free verse, dramatic monologue), stanza lengths, enjambment, caesura, where the volta falls, what the opening and closing images are. 3. Context — who is the poet? When did they write? What might their purpose be? Why does this matter for the poem's meaning? Always link everything to effect on the reader.
What is the difference between a 24-mark and an 8-mark unseen poetry question?

24-mark (Q1): detailed analysis of ONE poem. Language, structure, form, effect. Several developed points with close focus on specific words. 8-mark (Q2): COMPARISON of both poems. Focus on differences AND similarities in how both poets present the theme. Two developed comparative points, 1:1 evidence ratio, use comparative connectives (whereas, similarly, in contrast, unlike).
What makes a quotation worth analysing in poetry?

Choose quotations that are: short (one to five words ideally), contain a specific technique, carry rich connotations, and connect to the poem's central argument. Avoid long quotations — they waste time and the analysis becomes vague. The best quotes are single powerful words: a verb, a noun, an unexpected adjective. These give you the most to say.
What is the difference between tone and mood in poetry analysis?

Tone: the writer's or speaker's attitude towards their subject (e.g. bitter, elegiac, ironic, reverent). Mood: the emotional atmosphere created in the reader (e.g. unsettling, melancholy, claustrophobic). A poem can have a bitter tone but create a melancholic mood. Specify which you mean: 'the tone is... while the mood it creates is...'
What does CHRIS PRICE stand for and why use it?

CHRIS PRICE is a toolkit of analytical verbs for zooming in on language. C — Conveys. H — Highlights. R — Reinforces. I — Infers. S — Suggests. P — Portrays. R — Refers. I — Implies. C — Connotes. E — Evokes. Using these instead of 'shows' or 'makes' instantly elevates the precision of your analysis — e.g. 'the verb X connotes...' or 'the adjective X evokes a sense of...' Each verb carries a slightly different weight: 'connotes' is specific to word-level analysis; 'reinforces' implies something is building across the text; 'evokes' brings in sensory or emotional response.

CHRIS PRICE — analytical verbs: C = {{c1::Conveys}}, H = {{c2::Highlights}}, R = {{c3::Reinforces}}, I = {{c4::Infers}}, S = {{c5::Suggests}}, P = {{c6::Portrays}}, R = {{c7::Refers}}, I = {{c8::Implies}}, C = {{c9::Connotes}}, E = {{c10::Evokes}}. Use these instead of 'shows' to add {{c11::precision}} to your analysis.
What is the difference between a monologue and a dramatic monologue?

Monologue: one character speaks at length (may address other characters on stage). Dramatic monologue: a specific poetic form — one speaker addresses an implied, silent listener; the reader infers the situation. Crucially, the speaker often reveals more than they intend — the gap between what is said and what is implied is the point. E.g. My Last Duchess: the Duke addresses a visitor but unconsciously reveals his guilt.
What are the PERSUADER techniques for Paper 2 Question 5?

Personal Pronouns (direct address). Emotive language. Repetition and Rule of Three (tricolon). Statistics/Facts/Opinion as Fact. Use of authority figure ('scientists have proven'). Anecdote. Descriptive devices (similes, metaphors, adjectives). Exaggeration/hyperbole. Rhetorical question. Also: Synaesthesia (senses), Captatio Benevolentiae (appeal to goodwill), Praeteritio (mentioning something by pretending not to — 'I will not mention the appalling litter...').
What makes a Grade 8/9 viewpoint writing response?

Varied sentence types to affect/entertain (elongated sentences to ridicule). Patterns of language within sentences AND across paragraphs. Extended metaphor/semantic fields. Cyclical structure — mirroring opening and closing. Building persuasion through juxtaposition. You must give the impression this genuinely matters to you. Structural techniques: syntactic parallelism, contrast paragraphing, repeat paragraph lengths.
What are the forms for Paper 2 Question 5 viewpoint writing?
Letter, Letter to an editor (article style), Speech, Newspaper article, Magazine article, Blog. Linked to the theme of Section A texts. Plan 5-10 mins, write 30-35 mins, check 5 mins.
What are five approaches to opening a viewpoint piece?

1. Show the opposite side of your argument first. 2. Start anecdotally. 3. Pose a question answered later in the piece. 4. Start abstractly — something that later becomes a metaphor. 5. Be directly persuasive and emotive from the outset.
What are the five ways to use word choice in creative/language writing?

A — Word choice for tone. B — Word choice for sound (alliteration, sibilance, plosives). C — Word choice for syllable (rhythm, pace). D — Word choice with sentence/punctuation (short sentences for impact, dashes for interruption). E — Word choice for a pattern (semantic fields, motifs, repetition across paragraphs).
What are the key structural techniques for creative writing?

Syntactic parallelism (repeating grammatical structures). Cyclical structure (ending mirrors opening). Contrast paragraphing (juxtaposing two perspectives/settings). Repeated paragraph lengths (creates rhythm or disrupts it for effect).
What is the complete list of poetry terms your teacher highlighted?

Alliteration, Sibilance, Assonance, Consonance, Anaphora, Caesura, Enjambment, Volta (turning point), Onomatopoeia, Plosive, Oxymoron, Metaphor, Simile, Personification, Imagery, Juxtaposition, Dramatic monologue, Free verse, Half-rhyme, Stanza (not paragraph!), Tone, Voice, Symbolism, Metre, Rhythm, In media res, Irony, Polyphonic, Polysemous, Colloquial.
What is the difference between caesura, enjambment and volta?

Caesura: a pause within a line (often marked by punctuation) — creates tension or emphasis. Enjambment: a sentence runs over from one line to the next without a pause — creates flow, urgency or a sense of things not ending. Volta: the turning point of a poem where tone/argument shifts — often signals a change in understanding.
How should you approach an unseen poetry question?

Always analyse: Language (word choice, techniques, imagery), Structure (form, enjambment, stanzas, volta, rhyme scheme), and Context (poet's background, when written, why). Link EVERYTHING to the effect on the reader. For a comparison question: track through chronologically, maintain balance of evidence between poems, combine comparisons rather than writing separately.
What is the structure of AQA English Language Paper 1?

Section A: Reading — one literary fiction extract, 4 questions. Q1 (4 marks): list 4 things. Q2 (8 marks): language analysis. Q3 (8 marks): structural analysis. Q4 (20 marks): evaluate and comment on writer's methods. Section B: Writing — one creative writing task (narrative or description), 40 marks (24 content, 16 technical accuracy).

Paper 1 Q2 ({{c1::8}} marks) asks you to analyse {{c2::language}}. Paper 1 Q3 ({{c3::8}} marks) asks you to analyse {{c4::structure}}. Paper 1 Q4 ({{c5::20}} marks) asks you to {{c6::evaluate}} a statement about the text.
How should you structure a Paper 1 Q2 language analysis paragraph?

Identify the technique/word class. Quote specifically (zoom in on one word ideally). Analyse the connotations/effect. Link to the writer's intent. Use the word 'suggests', 'implies', 'conveys' rather than 'shows'. Aim for 3-4 developed points. Never just identify a technique — always analyse the effect.
What are the five ways to use word choice effectively in creative writing?

A — Tone: word choice creates a specific mood or atmosphere. B — Sound: alliteration, sibilance, plosives, onomatopoeia create sonic effects. C — Syllable: rhythm and pace controlled by word length (monosyllabic = blunt/fast, polysyllabic = flowing/slow). D — Sentence/punctuation: word placed at end of sentence for impact, short sentences for shock. E — Pattern: semantic fields, motifs, repetition across paragraphs create cohesion.
How does sentence length create effect in creative/analytical writing?

Short, monosyllabic sentences create shock, tension, finality (e.g. 'Waves hit.'). Long, complex sentences with multiple clauses create flow, overwhelm or breathlessness. Varied sentence length creates rhythm and controls the reader's pace through a text. Fragments (incomplete sentences) create immediacy and disorientation.

Using word choice for {{c1::sound}}: 'Spray surged...pushed and pressed...pounding waves, punching skywards' — the {{c2::alliterative}} 'p' creates a sense of {{c3::forceful impact}}. The verbs 'pushed and pressed' are {{c4::plosive}}, mimicking the physical force of the waves.

Using word choice for {{c2::tone}}: 'Weary and wounded, the train limped on, its gasping engine drawing {{c1::laboured}} breaths' — personification of the train as injured creates a tone of {{c3::suffering and exhaustion}}. The adjective 'laboured' has connotations of painful effort.
How should you approach Paper 1 Q3 (structural analysis)?

Think about: where the writer starts and ends (what changes?). Focus shifts — zooming in/out, moving between characters, time shifts. Turning points — where does the narrative shift? Sentence/paragraph length changes. Beginnings and endings of paragraphs — what is foregrounded, what is left until last? Always link structural choices to effect on the reader.
What structural techniques should you identify in Q3?

Cyclical structure (ending mirrors opening). In media res (starting mid-action). Narrative perspective shifts. Contrast paragraphing (juxtaposing two settings/ideas). Withholding information (building tension). Foregrounding (placing important element at start). Delayed revelation (revealing key information late). Syntactic parallelism (repeating grammatical structure).
What is cyclical structure and how does it work?

When the ending of a piece mirrors or echoes its opening — creating a sense of completion or irony. Example from the model: 'Kate's mind worked furiously, imagining the horrors' at the start mirrors 'Kate's mind worked furiously, remembering the joys' at the end — same phrase, opposite emotion. Shows transformation. Very effective for Q3 analysis and something to use in your own writing.
What is syntactic parallelism and what effect does it create?

Repeating the same grammatical structure across sentences or paragraphs. Example: 'His mother, though, had other ideas. His mother, though, decided... His mother, he sighed...' — the repetition builds frustration and emphasis. Creates rhythm, accumulates pressure. Useful in both analysis and writing.
What is contrast paragraphing?

Deliberately juxtaposing two contrasting descriptions, perspectives or settings in adjacent paragraphs. Example: 'The storm raged outside... The fire flickered inside.' Or: 'The usual crowd of commuters stalked across London... An eager mass of tourists raced across London' — same setting, opposite groups, opposite tones. Creates meaning through opposition.
How should you approach Paper 1 Q4 (evaluation)?

You are given a statement about how a student interpreted the text. You agree/disagree/partly agree using evidence. Structure: state your view clearly, use 3-4 pieces of evidence, analyse the methods, comment on effect. Use evaluative language: 'to a large extent', 'however', 'more convincingly', 'this is supported by'. Always refer to the writer's methods, not just what happens.
How is Paper 1 Section B creative writing marked?

40 marks total: 24 for content/organisation, 16 for technical accuracy (spelling, punctuation, grammar, vocabulary). You have a choice: either describe a picture prompt OR write a narrative using a given title/opening. Spend 45-50 mins on this including planning time.
What makes a high-level creative writing response for Paper 1?

Varied and deliberate sentence structures (fragments, complex, short for effect). Rich, specific vocabulary with connotations (not just 'big' — use 'looming', 'vast', 'towering'). A clear narrative or descriptive voice. Structural choices that mirror meaning (cyclical, contrast paragraphing). Controlled perspective and tone throughout. Techniques that serve the writing rather than just being ticked off a list.
What structural techniques can you use in your own creative writing?

Cyclical structure (begin and end with same/transformed image). In media res (start mid-action — 'Suddenly he awoke and was running'). Contrast paragraphing (storm outside vs calm inside). Syntactic parallelism (repeated grammatical structures for rhythm/emphasis). Withholding information (reveal key details late). Varying paragraph length for pace.
What vocabulary techniques create Grade 8/9 creative writing?

Use word choice for tone (precise adjectives and verbs, not generic ones). Use word choice for sound (alliteration, sibilance, plosives that mimic the described action). Use word choice for syllable (polysyllabic for flowing description, monosyllabic for shock). Use word choice for pattern (semantic fields that run through the whole piece, creating cohesion).

Model sentence types: '{{c1::Waves hit.}}' — short declarative for {{c2::impact}}. 'Weary and wounded, the train limped on, its gasping engine drawing laboured breaths, its wheels stumbling and shaking.' — long sentence with {{c3::personification}} for {{c4::sustained suffering}}. 'A flash – and another – and another –' — {{c5::dashes}} create {{c6::breathless, fragmented panic}}.
Give an example of contrast paragraphing technique from the model texts.

'The usual crowd of commuters stalked across London as the sun's nightly death shattered light over them. Set faces were grim — no smiles or joyous eyes. This was commuting hour, and it was hellish. / An eager mass of tourists raced across London as the sun's daily birth patched light over them. Expressive faces were excited — no frowns or tired eyes. This was visiting hour, and it was glorious.' — exact mirror structure, opposite emotions, powerful juxtaposition.
What techniques does the Q5 Market model text use?

Cyclical structure: 'Kate's mind worked furiously, imagining the horrors' mirrors 'Kate's mind worked furiously, remembering the joys' — same phrase, opposite emotion, shows transformation. Contrast paragraphing: palace (contained, sterile) vs marketplace (chaotic, alive). Semantic field of danger (assaulted, smacked, charged, chokehold, winded) vs semantic field of beauty (sparkling, delicate, exotic). Short sentences for impact: 'She could not settle. She could not stand still.' Parallelism: 'all dominating... all eradicating'.

Q5 Market model — cyclical structure: '{{c1::Kate's mind worked furiously, imagining the horrors}}' (opening — fear) mirrors '{{c2::Kate's mind worked furiously, remembering the joys}}' (closing — delight). Same phrase, opposite {{c3::emotion}} — shows the character's {{c4::transformation}} through the narrative.
What are the question-by-question timings for English Language Paper 1?

Start with 15 minutes reading and planning the whole paper. Q1 (4 marks): 5 min — identify 4 pieces of information from the extract. Q2 (8 marks): 10 min — analyse the writer's use of language. Q3 (8 marks): 10 min — analyse how the text is structured. Q4 (20 marks): 20 min — evaluate a statement about the text. Q5 Writing (24 marks content + 16 marks accuracy): 45 min total — 5 min planning, 35 min writing, 5 min checking.

Paper 1 timings: start with {{c1::15}} minutes reading and planning. Q1 = {{c2::4}} marks, {{c3::5}} minutes. Q2 = {{c4::8}} marks, {{c5::10}} minutes. Q3 = {{c6::8}} marks, {{c7::10}} minutes. Q4 = {{c8::20}} marks, {{c9::20}} minutes. Q5 Writing = {{c10::24}} marks content, {{c11::45}} minutes total.

Paper 1 Q5 Writing: spend {{c1::45}} minutes in total — {{c2::5}} minutes planning, {{c3::35}} minutes writing, {{c4::5}} minutes checking. Worth {{c5::24}} marks for content and {{c6::16}} marks for technical accuracy.

Paper 1 Q4 is worth {{c1::20}} marks and you should spend {{c2::20}} minutes on it. It asks you to {{c3::evaluate}} a statement about the text, using your own judgement and evidence. The key word in the question is always {{c4::'evaluate'}} or {{c5::'critically'}}.
What is the general exam timing rule and how does it apply to Language Paper 1?

A mark a minute is typically a good guide. Paper 1: Q1 (4 marks ≈ 4–5 min), Q2 (8 marks ≈ 8–10 min), Q3 (8 marks ≈ 8–10 min), Q4 (20 marks ≈ 20 min). Q5 Writing gets more time because planning and checking are built in — 45 min for 24 marks. Use the rule to check you are not overrunning on any single question.
What is the structure of AQA English Language Paper 2?

Section A: Reading — two non-fiction texts (one 19th century, one modern). Q1 (4 marks): identify true statements. Q2 (8 marks): summarise differences. Q3 (12 marks): language analysis on one text. Q4 (16 marks): compare writers' methods and perspectives across both texts. Section B: Writing — Q5, viewpoint/persuasion task, 40 marks (24 content, 16 technical accuracy).

Paper 2 Q3 ({{c1::12}} marks) asks you to analyse {{c2::language}} in one text. Q4 ({{c3::16}} marks) asks you to {{c4::compare}} both texts' methods and perspectives. Q5 ({{c5::40}} marks) is {{c6::viewpoint writing}}.
How should you approach Paper 2 Q2 (summarising differences)?

Identify differences in attitude, perspective or situation between the two texts. Use comparative connectives: 'whereas', 'in contrast', 'however', 'unlike'. Always quote from both texts. Don't just describe — identify the difference explicitly. Aim for 3-4 clear differences with supporting evidence from each text.
How should you approach Paper 2 Q4 (comparing perspectives)?

This is the highest-mark reading question (16 marks). Compare how each writer presents their view on the topic — their methods AND their perspectives. Structure: introduce both writers' overall stance, then work through evidence from both texts side by side. Use 'whereas', 'similarly', 'in contrast'. Comment on language, tone, structure AND purpose/audience. Quote both texts in every paragraph.
What does 'writers' methods' mean in Paper 2 Q4?

Methods = language choices (word choice, imagery, rhetoric), structural choices (how the text is organised, what comes first/last), tone (formal/informal, emotive/rational, humorous/serious), perspective (first person/third person, direct address), and purpose (to persuade, inform, argue, entertain). Always comment on WHY the writer made these choices — what effect do they have on the reader?
What are the PERSUADER techniques for Paper 2 Q5?

Personal Pronouns (direct address — 'you', 'we'). Emotive language. Repetition and Rule of Three (tricolon). Statistics/Facts/Opinion as Fact. Use of authority ('scientists have proven'). Anecdote. Descriptive devices (similes, metaphors, adjectives, adverbs). Exaggeration/hyperbole. Rhetorical questions. Also: Synaesthesia (appeal to senses), Captatio Benevolentiae (appeal to goodwill of audience), Praeteritio (talking about something by pretending not to — 'I will not mention...').
What are the forms for Paper 2 Q5 and how long should you spend?

Forms: Letter, Letter to an editor (article style), Speech, Newspaper article, Magazine article, Blog. Plan 5-10 mins. Write 30-35 mins. Check 5 mins. The form affects tone and conventions — a speech uses direct address and spoken rhythms; a newspaper article has a headline and formal tone; a blog is more conversational.
What features distinguish a Grade 7-9 viewpoint response from lower grades?

Varied sentence types to affect/entertain (elongated sentences to ridicule, short sentences to shock). Patterns of language within AND across paragraphs. Extended metaphor or semantic fields used consistently. Cyclical structure — opening and closing mirror each other. Building persuasion through juxtaposition. Humorous similes. The impression that this genuinely matters to the writer.
What are five ways to open a viewpoint writing piece?

1. Show the opposite side of your argument first, then undermine it. 2. Start anecdotally — a personal story that leads to your argument. 3. Pose a question that will be answered in your writing. 4. Start abstractly in a way that becomes a metaphor for your argument. 5. Be directly persuasive and emotive from the very first line.
How does the model viewpoint piece about plastic use PERSUADER techniques?

Rhetorical questions: 'Do you not feel ashamed to hear...? Are you not mortified to find...?' Statistics as fact: '1 million birds and over 100,000 sea mammals die every year'. Authority figure: 'David Attenborough has affirmed...'. Rule of three: 'whole habitats...whole species...whole countries'. Emotive language: 'scourges', 'destroying', 'choking'. Anaphora: 'Simply...Merely...Only...' builds momentum.

The model viewpoint piece uses {{c1::anaphora}}: 'Simply just turning a light off... {{c2::Merely}} picking up a bit of litter... {{c3::Only}} travelling on foot or bike...' — the repetition at the start of each sentence builds {{c4::momentum}} and makes the actions seem {{c5::easy and achievable}}.
How should you approach the Paper 2 Literature unseen poetry questions?

Q1 (24 marks): How does the poet present [theme/character]? Analyse language, structure, and form. Zoom in on specific words. Comment on effect on the reader. Q2 (8 marks): Compare two poems — identify similarities AND differences in theme AND method. Use comparative connectives. Maintain balance of evidence between both poems.
What is the difference between the 24-mark and 8-mark unseen poetry questions?

24-mark Q1: Detailed analysis of ONE poem — language, structure, form, effect. Similar approach to anthology analysis. 8-mark Q2: COMPARISON of both poems — focus on differences and similarities in how poets present the theme. Shorter — about 2 developed comparative points.

{{c1::Alliteration}}: first letter of words repeated. {{c2::Sibilance}}: repetition of 's' sounds. {{c3::Assonance}}: same vowel sound with different consonants. {{c4::Consonance}}: repetition of consonant sounds. {{c5::Anaphora}}: same word/phrase repeated at the start of successive lines.

{{c1::Caesura}}: a pause within a line. {{c2::Enjambment}}: sentence runs over from one line to next. {{c3::Volta}}: turning point in a poem. {{c4::In media res}}: starting mid-action. {{c5::Dramatic monologue}}: single speaker addressing an implied audience (not the poet).

{{c1::Oxymoron}}: two contradictory words together ('cold heat'). {{c2::Juxtaposition}}: contrasting ideas placed close together. {{c3::Personification}}: giving human qualities to objects/ideas. {{c4::Symbolism}}: object stands for something else. {{c5::Plosive}}: short burst of sound from b, d, g, k, p, t.

{{c1::Free verse}}: no rhyme scheme or regular rhythm. {{c2::Half-rhyme}}: similar but not identical end sounds. {{c3::Metre}}: arrangement of stressed/unstressed syllables. {{c4::Polysemous}}: a word or poem with many meanings. {{c5::Stanza}}: a group of lines in a poem — NOT a paragraph.
How do you analyse structure in an unseen poem?

Consider: number of stanzas and whether they're equal (regularity = control). Line length — long lines = flowing/overwhelming, short = fragmented/urgent. Rhyme scheme — strict = controlled/trapped, irregular = freedom/disorder. Enjambment across stanzas — ideas overflow, can't be contained. Where the volta falls — what changes? What is the first and last word/image — what does the poet foreground and end on?
What are the question-by-question timings for English Language Paper 2?

Start with 15 minutes reading and planning the whole paper. Q1 (8 marks): 8 min — identify 4 true statements from a list of 8. Q2 (12 marks): 12 min — write a summary using information from two texts. Q3 (16 marks): ~30 min — compare two writers' perspectives and methods. Q4 Writing (24 marks content + 16 marks accuracy): 45 min total — 5 min planning, 35 min writing, 5 min checking.

Paper 2 timings: start with {{c1::15}} minutes reading and planning. Q1 = {{c2::8}} marks, {{c3::8}} minutes (true/false). Q2 = {{c4::12}} marks, {{c5::12}} minutes (summary). Q3 = {{c6::16}} marks, {{c7::~30}} minutes (compare perspectives). Q4 Writing = {{c8::24}} marks content, {{c9::45}} minutes total.

Paper 2 Q1: identify {{c1::4}} true statements from a list of {{c2::8}}. Worth {{c3::8}} marks — spend {{c4::8}} minutes. Paper 2 Q2: write a {{c5::summary}} using information from {{c6::two texts}}. Worth {{c7::12}} marks — spend {{c8::12}} minutes.

Paper 2 Q3 is worth {{c1::16}} marks — spend {{c2::~30}} minutes comparing both writers' {{c3::perspectives and methods}}. Q4 Writing is worth {{c4::24}} marks (content) + {{c5::16}} marks (accuracy) — spend {{c6::45}} minutes total ({{c7::5}} planning, {{c8::35}} writing, {{c9::5}} checking).
What is the general exam timing rule and how does it apply to Language Paper 2?

A mark a minute is typically a good guide. Paper 2: Q1 (8 marks ≈ 8 min), Q2 (12 marks ≈ 12 min), Q3 (16 marks ≈ 16–30 min). Q4 Writing gets more time than marks suggest — 45 min for 24 marks — because planning and checking are essential. If you are spending significantly more than a minute per mark on any reading question, you are overrunning.
What checklist should you work through when annotating for Paper 2 Q3?

1. Pronoun: 1st person (personal/emotive) or 3rd person (authoritative/detached)? 2. Direct address: 'you' — creates intimacy or challenge. 3. Figurative devices: metaphor, simile, personification — what does each suggest? 4. Factual devices: statistics, proper nouns, named authorities — builds credibility. 5. Syntax: long sentences (elaborate/overwhelming) vs short (blunt/emphatic). 6. Sentence types: declarative, interrogative, exclamatory, imperative. 7. Repetition/anaphora: what idea is being driven home?
What is the paragraph framework for Paper 2 Q4?

(1) State both writers' perspectives on this point. (2) Quote and analyse Text 1 — technique + effect. (3) Quote and analyse Text 2 — same point. (4) Compare explicitly: 'Whereas Writer A... Writer B...' Every paragraph must reference BOTH texts. End by linking methods back to each writer's overall purpose. Never write all of Text 1 then all of Text 2 — interweave throughout.
What evaluative vocabulary should you use in Paper 2 Q4?

Instead of 'similarly' and 'also', use: notably, markedly, significantly, chiefly, primarily, indeed, strikingly, particularly, evidently, crucially. For contrast: conversely, by contrast, in opposition to, whereas, unlike. For degree: 'to a greater extent', 'more emphatically than', 'less forcefully'. These signal evaluation — judging the significance of methods — rather than mere description.
How should you use the reading time at the start of Paper 2?

Read both texts actively — annotate as you go. Mark: perspective (what does each writer believe?), methods (figurative, factual, structural), tone shifts, repeated ideas. Underline 2-3 quotes for Q3 and 3-4 per text for Q4. Note purpose and audience for each text. Don't re-read from scratch for each question — use your annotations. For Q4, decide your overall comparison before you start writing.
{{c1::Alliteration}}: the first letter of a word is repeated in words that follow. E.g. '{{c2::cold, crisp, clear ice}}'. When the repeated sound is 's' or 'sh', this is called {{c3::sibilance}}.
{{c1::Anaphora}}: the repetition of the same {{c2::words or phrases at the beginning}} of lines of poetry. Creates emphasis and rhythm.
{{c1::Assonance}}: the same {{c2::vowel sound}} is repeated but the consonants are different. E.g. '{{c3::he passed her a sharp, dark glance}}'.
{{c1::Caesura}}: a {{c2::pause}} in a line of poetry, often marked by punctuation. Creates tension, emphasis or a sense of interruption.
{{c1::Colloquial}}: sounding like {{c2::spoken speech}}, usually with an {{c3::informal}} meaning. Creates a sense of authenticity or character voice.
{{c1::Consonance}}: the repetition of {{c2::consonant sounds}} in nearby words. Similar to alliteration but not restricted to the first letter.
{{c1::Dramatic monologue}}: a poem that uses the assumed voice of a {{c2::single speaker who is not the poet}}, addressing an implied audience. E.g. My Last Duchess.
{{c1::Enjambment}}: when a sentence or phrase {{c2::runs over from one line to the next}} without a pause. Creates flow, urgency or a sense of things not ending.
{{c1::Form}}: the {{c2::type of poem}} and its features — number of lines, rhyme scheme, structure. E.g. a {{c3::sonnet}} has 14 lines, traditionally used for love.
{{c1::Free verse}}: poetry that {{c2::doesn't rhyme}} and has no regular {{c3::rhythm or line length}}. Can suggest freedom, chaos or natural speech.
{{c1::Half-rhyme}} (pararhyme): words that have a {{c2::similar but not identical}} end sound. E.g. '{{c3::crisp and grasp}}'. Creates a sense of discord or unease.
{{c1::Imagery}}: similes, metaphors and personification — they all compare something '{{c2::real}}' with something '{{c3::imagined}}'. Creates vivid pictures in the reader's mind.
{{c1::In media res}}: when a narrative starts {{c2::in the middle of the action}}. Creates immediacy and disorientation. E.g. Bayonet Charge: '{{c3::Suddenly, he was awake and running}}'.
{{c1::Irony}}: using words to imply the {{c2::opposite}} of what they normally mean. Can also describe a difference between what is expected and what happens.
{{c1::Juxtaposition}}: placing two contrasting {{c2::ideas, characters or images}} close together to invite the reader to contrast them.
{{c1::Metaphor}}: a word or phrase used to imply {{c2::figurative}} resemblance — NOT literal. E.g. '{{c3::he flew into the room}}'.
{{c1::Metre}}: the arrangement of {{c2::stressed and unstressed syllables}} to create a particular rhythm. E.g. iambic pentameter = da-DUM × 5.
{{c1::Onomatopoeia}}: a word that {{c2::sounds like}} the noise it describes. E.g. '{{c3::splash, bang, pop, hiss}}'.
