Speciation is the formation of a new species. Occurs when a population is split by a geographical barrier (e.g. mountain, sea). The two groups are isolated — no interbreeding. Each group faces different selection pressures → different mutations become advantageous → allele frequencies change differently in each population → over many generations, the two populations become so genetically different they can no longer interbreed successfully — two distinct species exist. Example: Darwin's finches on different Galapagos islands evolved different beak shapes to suit different food sources.

children.name: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table

fields: An atom contains: {{c1::protons}} (positive, in nucleus), {{c2::neutrons}} (neutral, in nucleus), {{c3::electrons}} (negative, in shells). Atomic number = number of {{c4::protons}}. Mass number = number of {{c5::protons + neutrons}}.

Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of {{c1::protons}} but different numbers of {{c2::neutrons}}. E.g. Carbon-12 and Carbon-14. Same chemical properties, different {{c3::mass}}.
How is the periodic table organised?

Elements arranged in order of atomic number. Vertical columns = groups (elements with same number of outer electrons, similar properties). Horizontal rows = periods (elements with same number of electron shells). Metals on left, non-metals on right, separated by a zigzag line.
What are the properties of Group 1 (alkali metals) and how do they change down the group?

React with water to produce hydrogen gas and a metal hydroxide (alkaline solution). React with oxygen to form metal oxides. Going down Group 1: increased reactivity (outer electron further from nucleus, easier to lose), lower melting/boiling points, less dense.
What are the properties of Group 7 (halogens)?

Non-metals that exist as diatomic molecules (F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂). Going down: less reactive (harder to gain an electron), higher melting/boiling points, darker colour (F=pale yellow, Cl=green, Br=orange/brown, I=grey solid/purple vapour). More reactive halogens displace less reactive ones from solutions.
What are the properties of Group 0 (noble gases)?

Inert (unreactive) because they have full outer electron shells. Monatomic (exist as single atoms). Going down: higher boiling points (stronger intermolecular forces). Used in filament bulbs (Ar), balloons (He), discharge lighting (Ne/Ar).

Early atomic models: Thomson's 'plum pudding' — {{c1::positive sphere with embedded electrons}}. Rutherford's gold foil experiment discovered the {{c2::nucleus}} — most of an atom is {{c3::empty space}}. Bohr's model: electrons in {{c4::fixed shells (orbits)}} around the nucleus.

Electron configuration: first shell holds up to {{c1::2}} electrons, second shell up to {{c2::8}}, third shell up to {{c3::8}} (for elements 1–20). Sodium (atomic number 11): {{c4::2,8,1}}. Chlorine (atomic number 17): {{c5::2,8,7}}. Calcium (atomic number 20): {{c6::2,8,8,2}}.
What are the trends down Group 1 (alkali metals) in reactivity, melting point and appearance?

Going down Group 1 (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs):
• Reactivity increases: outer electron is in a higher shell, further from nucleus, less strongly attracted → easier to lose → more reactive. Shielding from inner electrons also increases.
• Melting and boiling points decrease (unusual — softer metals)
• Density increases
• React with water: Li — fizzes gently; Na — fizzes vigorously, may catch fire; K — catches fire immediately with lilac flame.
All reactions with water: metal + water → metal hydroxide + hydrogen. E.g. 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂
What are the trends down Group 7 (halogens) in reactivity and physical state?

Going down Group 7 (F, Cl, Br, I):
• Reactivity decreases: gaining an electron becomes harder as the outer shell is further from the nucleus, with more inner electron shells providing greater shielding.
• Physical state at room temperature: F₂ = pale yellow gas; Cl₂ = green gas; Br₂ = orange/brown liquid; I₂ = grey solid (purple vapour)
• Melting/boiling points increase (stronger intermolecular forces)
Displacement: Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂ (Cl₂ displaces Br⁻ because Cl is more reactive — solution turns orange-brown).
What are the transition metals and what properties do they share?

The transition metals are found in the central block (periods 4–7, groups 3–12) of the periodic table — e.g. iron, copper, nickel, chromium, titanium.
Properties: high melting and boiling points (except mercury — liquid at room temperature). High density. Good conductors of heat and electricity. Hard and strong (except Hg). Form coloured compounds (e.g. CuSO₄ = blue, FeCl₃ = orange). Can act as catalysts (iron in Haber process, nickel in hydrogenation). Often have multiple oxidation states (e.g. Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺).
What is ionic bonding?

Transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal. Metal loses electrons → positive ion (cation). Non-metal gains electrons → negative ion (anion). Ions arranged in a giant ionic lattice held together by strong electrostatic forces. High melting points. Conduct electricity when molten or dissolved (ions free to move).
What is covalent bonding?

Sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms. Each shared pair = one covalent bond. Can form simple molecular structures (low melting points, don't conduct electricity) or giant covalent structures (very high melting points, e.g. diamond, graphite, silicon dioxide).

Diamond: each carbon forms {{c1::4}} covalent bonds in a {{c2::giant covalent lattice}}. Very hard, high melting point, {{c3::doesn't conduct electricity}} (no free electrons). Graphite: each carbon forms {{c4::3}} bonds in layers — has {{c5::delocalised electrons}} and {{c6::conducts electricity}}.

Graphene: a single layer of {{c1::graphite}} — one atom thick. Excellent {{c2::electrical conductor}}. Fullerenes (e.g. {{c3::Buckminsterfullerene C₆₀}}): carbon atoms arranged in hollow {{c4::spheres or tubes}}. Used in drug delivery and nanotechnology.
What is metallic bonding?

Lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised (free) electrons. Strong electrostatic attraction between ions and electrons. This explains: high melting points (strong bonds), electrical conductivity (free electrons carry charge), malleability (layers of ions can slide — electrons maintain bonding), thermal conductivity.
What are alloys and why are they harder than pure metals?

Alloys are mixtures of metals (or a metal with another element). Different-sized atoms disrupt the regular lattice, making it harder for layers to slide over each other. Examples: steel (iron + carbon), brass (copper + zinc), bronze (copper + tin).

Properties of simple molecular substances: {{c1::low}} melting and boiling points (weak {{c2::intermolecular forces}} between molecules — not covalent bonds). {{c3::Don't conduct electricity}} (no ions or free electrons). E.g. water, CO₂, ammonia.
What are polymers and how are they formed?

Long chain molecules made by joining many small monomer units in polymerisation. Monomers must have a double bond (alkenes). Many polymers come from crude oil. Properties depend on the monomer and conditions used. Generally: strong, chemically unreactive, solid at room temperature, don't conduct electricity.
What are nanoparticles and why are they different from bulk materials?

Nanoparticles are particles with dimensions between 1 and 100 nanometres (1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m). At this scale, the proportion of surface atoms is very large compared to bulk material.
Why different: extremely large surface area to volume ratio → greatly enhanced reactivity and different properties. Quantum effects also become significant.
Uses: silver nanoparticles (antibacterial — socks, wound dressings), titanium oxide (sunscreen), carbon nanotubes (strong lightweight materials, electronics), gold nanoparticles (targeted drug delivery, cancer treatment).
Concerns: very small size means they can penetrate cell membranes and accumulate in tissues — long-term health effects unknown.

Comparing structures and conductivity: ionic compounds conduct electricity when {{c1::molten or dissolved}} (ions free to move) but not when {{c2::solid}} (ions fixed). Metals conduct due to {{c3::delocalised electrons}}. Graphite/graphene conduct due to {{c4::delocalised electrons}} between layers. Diamond does {{c5::NOT}} conduct (no free electrons or ions). Simple molecular substances do {{c6::NOT}} conduct (no ions or free electrons).
What is the reactivity series and why is it useful?

Potassium, Sodium, Lithium, Calcium, Magnesium, Aluminium, Carbon, Zinc, Iron, Tin, Lead, Hydrogen, Copper, Silver, Gold, Platinum (most to least reactive). More reactive metals displace less reactive ones from solutions. Carbon can reduce metal oxides above it in the series.

Displacement reaction: {{c1::zinc}} + copper sulfate → {{c2::zinc sulfate}} + {{c3::copper}}. Zinc is more reactive so it {{c4::displaces}} copper. This is also a {{c5::redox}} reaction — zinc is {{c6::oxidised}} (loses electrons), copper is {{c7::reduced}} (gains electrons).
How is aluminium extracted and why is this method used?

Aluminium is extracted by electrolysis of molten aluminium oxide (bauxite) because it is too reactive to be reduced by carbon. Very energy-intensive and expensive. The cathode (negative) attracts Al³⁺ ions which gain electrons (reduction). The anode (positive) attracts O²⁻ ions which lose electrons (oxidation) — oxygen released.
How is iron extracted in the blast furnace?

Iron ore (haematite, Fe₂O₃) + coke (carbon) + limestone in a blast furnace. Hot air blasted in — coke burns to form CO₂, then CO. CO reduces iron oxide: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. Limestone (CaCO₃) removes acidic impurities as slag. Molten iron collected at bottom.
What is electrolysis and what happens at each electrode?

Uses electricity to break down ionic compounds. Only works when ions are free to move (molten or dissolved). Cathode (negative): positive ions gain electrons — reduction. Anode (positive): negative ions lose electrons — oxidation. OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain.

Electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution): cathode produces {{c1::hydrogen}} gas. Anode produces {{c2::chlorine}} gas. Remaining solution becomes {{c3::sodium hydroxide}}. Products used for: bleach ({{c4::Cl₂ + NaOH}}), plastics ({{c5::Cl₂}}), cleaning agents ({{c6::NaOH}}).
What happens during electrolysis of a molten ionic compound?

When an ionic compound is molten, ions are free to move. Only two ions present — both discharge. Example: molten lead bromide (PbBr₂). Cathode (−): Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb (lead metal deposited). Anode (+): 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻ (bromine gas released). Molten compounds are simpler than aqueous — no water, no competition between ions. Another example: molten aluminium oxide in Hall-Héroult process — Al³⁺ discharged at cathode (aluminium metal), O²⁻ discharged at anode (oxygen gas).

Electrolysis of molten lead bromide: at the cathode, {{c1::Pb²⁺}} ions gain {{c2::2 electrons}} → {{c3::lead metal}} deposited. At the anode, {{c4::2Br⁻}} ions lose {{c5::2 electrons}} → {{c6::bromine gas}} released. Molten compounds only contain {{c7::two types of ion}} — no water means no {{c8::competition}}.
What are the rules for predicting products of electrolysis of aqueous solutions?

In aqueous solution, water provides competing H⁺ and OH⁻ ions. CATHODE (reduction): if the metal is BELOW hydrogen in the reactivity series → metal deposited. If the metal is ABOVE hydrogen → hydrogen gas produced instead (H⁺ discharged). ANODE (oxidation): if halide ions (Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻) are present in HIGH concentration → halogen gas produced. Otherwise → oxygen gas produced from OH⁻ ions (from water). Examples: CuSO₄(aq) — cathode: Cu (copper below H); anode: O₂ (no halide). NaCl(aq) — cathode: H₂ (sodium above H); anode: Cl₂ (high Cl⁻ concentration).

Electrolysis of aqueous solutions — cathode rule: if the metal is {{c1::below hydrogen}} in the reactivity series, {{c2::metal}} is deposited. If the metal is {{c3::above hydrogen}}, {{c4::hydrogen gas}} is produced. Anode rule: if {{c5::halide ions}} (Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻) are present in high concentration, {{c6::halogen gas}} is produced. Otherwise {{c7::oxygen}} is produced from {{c8::OH⁻}} ions in water.
What happens when acids react with metals, bases and carbonates?

Acid + metal → salt + hydrogen. Acid + base → salt + water (neutralisation). Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂. The name of the salt depends on the acid: HCl → chloride, H₂SO₄ → sulfate, HNO₃ → nitrate.

The pH scale runs from {{c1::0}} to {{c2::14}}. pH {{c3::below 7}} = acidic, pH {{c4::7}} = neutral, pH {{c5::above 7}} = alkaline. Strong acids (e.g. HCl) fully {{c6::ionise}} in water. Weak acids (e.g. ethanoic acid) only {{c7::partially ionise}}.
[HT only] What are half equations and how do you write them for electrolysis?

Half equations show what happens at each electrode during electrolysis.
At cathode (reduction — gain of electrons):
Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper deposited)
2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ (hydrogen gas)
At anode (oxidation — loss of electrons):
2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻ (chlorine gas)
4OH⁻ → 2H₂O + O₂ + 4e⁻ (oxygen gas from water)
Key rule: electrons appear on the right of anode equations and on the left of cathode equations. Balance charges, then atoms.
What happens during the electrolysis of copper sulfate solution with copper electrodes? Why is this used in electroplating?

With copper electrodes: copper dissolves from the anode (Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻) and is deposited at the cathode (Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu). The concentration of CuSO₄ stays constant — pure copper transferred from anode to cathode.
Purification of copper: impure copper anode dissolves, pure copper deposited on cathode.
Electroplating: object to plate = cathode. Plating metal = anode. Electrolyte contains ions of the plating metal. Used to: protect against corrosion (chromium-plated car parts), improve appearance (gold-plated jewellery), reduce costs (use cheap metal coated with silver).
[HT only] How do you write ionic equations and what are spectator ions?

Ionic equations show only the ions/atoms that actually react — spectator ions are not included.
Spectator ions: ions present in solution that don't change during the reaction.
Example: HCl(aq) + NaOH(aq) → NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l)
Full ionic equation: H⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) + Na⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) + H₂O(l)
Spectator ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) cancel out → net ionic equation: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l)
This is the ionic equation for ANY neutralisation of strong acid and base.
What is the difference between exothermic and endothermic reactions?

Exothermic: releases energy to surroundings — temperature rises. Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation, respiration, hand warmers. Endothermic: absorbs energy from surroundings — temperature falls. Products have more energy. Examples: photosynthesis, thermal decomposition, sports injury packs.
How do you interpret reaction profile (energy level) diagrams?

X-axis = reaction progress. Y-axis = energy. Reactants on left, products on right. Exothermic: products lower than reactants (overall energy released). Endothermic: products higher than reactants. Activation energy = energy needed to start the reaction (the 'hump'). A catalyst lowers activation energy.

[HT only] Bond energy calculations: energy is required to {{c1::break}} bonds (endothermic). Energy is released when bonds {{c2::form}} (exothermic). Overall energy change = energy to {{c3::break bonds}} − energy {{c4::released forming bonds}}. If result is negative, reaction is {{c5::exothermic}}.
[HT only] Worked example: calculate the energy change for H₂ + Cl₂ → 2HCl. Bond energies: H–H = 436 kJ/mol, Cl–Cl = 243 kJ/mol, H–Cl = 432 kJ/mol.

Energy to break bonds (in): H–H = 436, Cl–Cl = 243. Total in = 679 kJ/mol.
Energy released forming bonds (out): 2 × H–Cl = 2 × 432 = 864 kJ/mol.
Overall energy change = 679 − 864 = −185 kJ/mol.
Negative = exothermic (energy released to surroundings).
What is the required practical for energy changes? What is measured and why use a polystyrene cup?

Mix two solutions (e.g. acid + alkali, or dissolving a salt) in a polystyrene cup. Record temperature of each solution before mixing. Mix and record maximum (or minimum for endothermic) temperature.
Polystyrene cup: low thermal conductivity = less heat lost to surroundings = more accurate result.
Variables: volume and concentration of solutions, starting temperature, mass of solid.
Worked example: neutralisation — temperature rises (exothermic). Dissolving NH₄Cl — temperature falls (endothermic).
What are hydrogen fuel cells and what are their advantages and disadvantages?

A fuel cell uses hydrogen and oxygen to produce electricity directly via a chemical reaction: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O.
Advantages: only product is water (no CO₂ during use), more efficient than combustion engines, no moving parts, quiet.
Disadvantages: hydrogen difficult to store and transport safely (explosive, low density), most hydrogen currently made from fossil fuels (so not truly zero carbon), infrastructure lacking, expensive to produce.

Activation energy: the {{c1::minimum energy}} required for particles to react when they collide. A {{c2::catalyst}} lowers the activation energy by providing an {{c3::alternative reaction pathway}}. On a reaction profile, a catalyst is shown as a {{c4::lower hump}} — the reactants and products are at the {{c5::same energy levels}} as without catalyst.
What is the difference between an exothermic and endothermic reaction on an energy profile diagram?

Exothermic: products are at a LOWER energy level than reactants. The difference (reactants − products) = energy released. Activation energy hump starts from reactants level.
Endothermic: products are at a HIGHER energy level than reactants. Energy must be continuously supplied. Both show: reactants on left, activation energy peak, products on right.
Key mark scheme point: the activation energy is measured from the reactants to the PEAK (top of hump), not from reactants to products.
Why is the reaction between zinc and copper sulfate solution described as exothermic, and what would you observe?

Zinc displaces copper: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu.
It is exothermic — energy is released as new bonds (Zn–O in ZnSO₄) are stronger than bonds broken (Cu–O in CuSO₄).
Observations: blue solution turns colourless/pale, orange/pink copper metal appears on zinc surface, temperature of solution increases.
Exam tip: always state what you would measure (temperature with thermometer) and what change indicates exothermic (temperature rise).
Classify these as exothermic or endothermic: (a) burning methane (b) dissolving ammonium nitrate (c) hand warmers (d) photosynthesis (e) neutralisation of HCl with NaOH.

(a) Burning methane — exothermic (combustion always releases energy)
(b) Dissolving ammonium nitrate — endothermic (temperature falls, used in cold packs)
(c) Hand warmers — exothermic (iron oxidation releases heat)
(d) Photosynthesis — endothermic (absorbs light energy to build glucose)
(e) Neutralisation HCl + NaOH — exothermic (temperature rises ~6–8°C)
Relative formula mass (Mr): sum of relative atomic masses (Ar) of all atoms in the formula. E.g. H₂O: (2×{{c1::1}}) + {{c2::16}} = {{c3::18}}. CO₂: 12 + (2×16) = {{c4::44}}.

1 mole of a substance contains {{c1::6.02 × 10²³}} particles (Avogadro's constant). Moles = {{c2::mass (g)}} ÷ {{c3::Mr}}. If Mr of NaCl = 58.5 and you have 11.7g: moles = 11.7÷58.5 = {{c4::0.2 mol}}.
What is percentage yield and why is it rarely 100%?
% yield = (actual yield ÷ theoretical yield) × 100. Never 100% because: reaction may be reversible (doesn't go to completion), some product lost during separation/purification, side reactions occur.
What is atom economy and why does it matter?

Atom economy = (Mr of desired product ÷ total Mr of all products) × 100. Measures how much reactant mass ends up in the desired product. High atom economy = less waste = more sustainable and cost-effective.

The law of conservation of mass: mass of {{c1::reactants}} = mass of {{c2::products}}. If mass appears to change in an experiment, it is because a {{c3::gas}} has been produced or has been used from the air (e.g. burning magnesium gains mass from {{c4::oxygen}}).
Worked example: reacting masses from a balanced equation. What mass of water is produced from 4g of hydrogen? (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O)

Step 1: Mr of H₂ = 2, Mr of H₂O = 18.
Step 2: From equation, 2 mol H₂ → 2 mol H₂O (1:1 ratio).
Step 3: moles of H₂ = 4 ÷ 2 = 2 mol.
Step 4: moles of H₂O = 2 mol.
Step 5: mass of H₂O = 2 × 18 = 36 g.
Shortcut: mass ratio from equation = 4:36. Scale to given mass: 4g H₂ → 36g H₂O.

Concentration in mol/dm³ = {{c1::moles of solute}} ÷ {{c2::volume of solution in dm³}}. 1 dm³ = {{c3::1000 cm³}}. To convert cm³ to dm³, {{c4::divide by 1000}}. Example: 0.5 mol NaCl in 2 dm³ → concentration = {{c5::0.25 mol/dm³}}.
How do you calculate concentration in g/dm³ and convert between g/dm³ and mol/dm³?

Concentration (g/dm³) = mass of solute (g) ÷ volume of solution (dm³)
To convert mol/dm³ → g/dm³: multiply by Mr.
To convert g/dm³ → mol/dm³: divide by Mr.
Example: 0.25 mol/dm³ NaCl (Mr = 58.5): concentration in g/dm³ = 0.25 × 58.5 = 14.625 g/dm³.
[HT only] Worked example: calculate the concentration of NaOH, given 25.0 cm³ of NaOH required 23.50 cm³ of 0.100 mol/dm³ HCl for neutralisation. (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O)

Step 1: moles of HCl = 0.100 × (23.50 ÷ 1000) = 0.00235 mol.
Step 2: ratio HCl:NaOH = 1:1, so moles NaOH = 0.00235 mol.
Step 3: concentration NaOH = 0.00235 ÷ (25.0 ÷ 1000) = 0.094 mol/dm³.
Exam tip: always convert cm³ to dm³ (÷1000) before using in concentration formula.
[HT only] What is a limiting reactant? How do you identify it?

The limiting reactant is the one that is completely used up first — it limits the amount of product that can form. The other reactant is in excess (some remains at the end).
Method: calculate moles of each reactant → compare to the equation ratio → the one that runs out first is limiting.
Example: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. 3 mol H₂ and 2 mol O₂: need 1.5 mol O₂ for 3 mol H₂ → O₂ is in excess. H₂ is limiting.
Worked example: percentage yield. Theoretical yield of NaCl = 5.85g. Actual yield from experiment = 4.68g. What is the percentage yield?

% yield = (actual yield ÷ theoretical yield) × 100
= (4.68 ÷ 5.85) × 100 = 80%
Reasons yield is less than 100%: reaction may be reversible (not complete), product lost during filtering/evaporation/transfer, side reactions producing different products.

(Higher Tier) Molar volume of a gas at room temperature and pressure (RTP): 1 mole of any gas = {{c1::24 dm³}} (24,000 cm³). Volume (dm³) = moles × {{c2::24}}. Moles = volume (dm³) ÷ {{c3::24}}. Example: 2g H₂ (Mr=2) = 1 mol → volume = {{c4::24 dm³}}.
(Higher Tier) Calculate the volume of CO₂ produced when 10g of CaCO₃ reacts with excess HCl. (CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂, Mr CaCO₃ = 100)

Step 1: moles CaCO₃ = 10 ÷ 100 = 0.1 mol.
Step 2: from equation, 1 mol CaCO₃ → 1 mol CO₂ (1:1 ratio).
Step 3: moles CO₂ = 0.1 mol.
Step 4: volume CO₂ = 0.1 × 24 = 2.4 dm³ (or 2400 cm³).
Remember: only works at room temperature and pressure (RTP).
What is the required practical for Quantitative Chemistry? What is the method for an acid-alkali titration?

Method: fill burette with acid. Pipette 25.0 cm³ of alkali into conical flask. Add a few drops of indicator (phenolphthalein — pink in alkali, colourless in acid). Run acid from burette slowly, swirling constantly. Stop when indicator just changes colour (endpoint). Record volume added (titre).
Repeat to get concordant results (within 0.10 cm³).
Mean titre = average of concordant results (exclude anomalies).
Accuracy tip: rinse burette with acid before filling; use white tile to see colour change clearly.

Atom economy = (Mr of {{c1::desired product(s)}} ÷ {{c2::total Mr of all products}}) × 100. High atom economy means less {{c3::waste}} and is more {{c4::sustainable}}. Addition reactions have atom economy of {{c5::100%}} because only one product forms.
How do you test for hydrogen, oxygen, CO₂ and chlorine gas?
Hydrogen: burning splint → squeaky pop. Oxygen: glowing splint → relights. CO₂: limewater → turns milky/cloudy. Chlorine: damp litmus paper → bleaches it white.
What is paper chromatography used for and how do you calculate Rf?

Identifies substances in a mixture by how far they travel up chromatography paper. Rf = distance travelled by substance ÷ distance travelled by solvent. Pure substances give one spot. Can be used to identify unknown substances by comparing Rf values.
What is a pure substance and what is a formulation?

Pure substance: only one type of element or compound — has sharp melting/boiling point. Formulation: deliberately designed mixture with specific proportions for a purpose (e.g. medicines, fuels, paints, fertilisers, foods). Impure substances melt/boil over a range of temperatures.

Flame test colours: Lithium = {{c1::crimson red}}. Sodium = {{c2::yellow/orange}}. Potassium = {{c3::lilac}}. Calcium = {{c4::orange-red}}. Copper = {{c5::green/blue}}. The test is done by placing the sample on a {{c6::nichrome wire loop}} in a {{c7::Bunsen flame}}.
How do you test for carbonate ions (CO₃²⁻)?

Add dilute acid (e.g. dilute HCl) to the solid or solution. If carbonate ions are present, fizzing occurs and carbon dioxide gas is released.
CO₃²⁻ + 2H⁺ → CO₂ + H₂O
Confirm the gas is CO₂ by passing through limewater — turns milky/cloudy.

Testing for halide ions — add dilute nitric acid then silver nitrate solution (AgNO₃):
Cl⁻ gives a {{c1::white}} precipitate (AgCl).
Br⁻ gives a {{c2::cream}} precipitate (AgBr).
I⁻ gives a {{c3::yellow}} precipitate (AgI).
Dilute HNO₃ added first to remove {{c4::carbonate ions}} that would interfere.
How do you test for sulfate ions (SO₄²⁻)?

Add dilute hydrochloric acid (to remove interfering ions such as carbonates) then add barium chloride solution (BaCl₂).
If sulfate ions are present: a white precipitate of barium sulfate (BaSO₄) forms — insoluble in acid.
Ba²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → BaSO₄ (white precipitate)
The HCl ensures any carbonate precipitate (which is also white) would dissolve, confirming the result is truly from sulfate.

Testing for metal ions by adding NaOH solution drop by drop — precipitate colour:
Cu²⁺: {{c1::blue}} precipitate. Fe²⁺: {{c2::green}} precipitate. Fe³⁺: {{c3::brown/rust}} precipitate. Ca²⁺: {{c4::white}} precipitate. Al³⁺: white precipitate that {{c5::dissolves in excess NaOH}} (amphoteric).
How do you test for ammonium ions (NH₄⁺)?

Add sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH) and gently warm. If ammonium ions are present, ammonia gas (NH₄⁺ + OH⁻ → NH₃ + H₂O) is released — has a distinctive pungent smell.
Confirm with damp red litmus paper → turns blue (ammonia is alkaline).
What is the required practical for chromatography? What are the key technique points?

Method: draw a pencil baseline (not pen — ink dissolves) on chromatography paper. Spot samples onto the line using a capillary tube. Allow spots to dry. Stand paper in solvent below the baseline. Allow solvent to rise. Remove before solvent reaches top. Mark solvent front immediately.
Calculate Rf = distance moved by spot ÷ distance moved by solvent front.
Key points: use pencil not pen; keep solvent below baseline; don't move paper while running; cover to prevent evaporation.
What is instrumental analysis and what are its advantages over chemical tests?

Flame emission spectroscopy: elements identified by the specific wavelengths of light they emit when heated. Produces a unique line spectrum for each element. Used to identify metal ions in solution.
Mass spectrometry: identifies substances by their mass-to-charge ratio. Can identify very small amounts of unknown substances.
Advantages over chemical tests: more sensitive (detect tiny quantities), more accurate, faster results, can identify substances that give similar chemical test results.
How would you identify an unknown ionic compound using a systematic sequence of tests?

Step 1: Flame test → identifies metal cation (Na = yellow, K = lilac, Ca = orange-red, Cu = green/blue, Li = crimson).
Step 2: Add NaOH → precipitate colour identifies other metal ions (Cu²⁺ blue, Fe²⁺ green, Fe³⁺ brown, Al³⁺ white dissolves in excess).
Step 3: Add dilute acid → fizzes = carbonate (CO₃²⁻) present; test gas with limewater.
Step 4: Add AgNO₃ after HNO₃ → white/cream/yellow ppt = Cl⁻/Br⁻/I⁻.
Step 5: Add BaCl₂ after HCl → white ppt = sulfate (SO₄²⁻).
An unknown compound gives a yellow flame test. Adding NaOH gives no precipitate. Adding AgNO₃ (after HNO₃) gives a cream precipitate. Identify the compound.

Yellow flame → sodium (Na⁺) ion present.
No precipitate with NaOH → no copper, iron or aluminium ions (confirms Na⁺).
Cream precipitate with AgNO₃ → bromide (Br⁻) ion present (AgBr is cream).
Compound = sodium bromide (NaBr).
Exam tip: always name BOTH the cation AND anion to fully identify an ionic compound.
What is crude oil and how is it separated?

Crude oil is a mixture of hydrocarbons (compounds of C and H only). Separated by fractional distillation — heated, vapours rise through a fractionating column, condense at different temperatures (different boiling points). Shorter chain = lower boiling point = more flammable = less viscous.

Alkanes: general formula {{c1::CₙH₂ₙ₊₂}}. Saturated hydrocarbons — only {{c2::single}} C-C bonds. First four: {{c3::methane (CH₄)}}, {{c4::ethane (C₂H₆)}}, {{c5::propane (C₃H₈)}}, {{c6::butane (C₄H₁₀)}}.

Alkenes: general formula {{c1::CₙH₂ₙ}}. Unsaturated — contain a {{c2::C=C double bond}}. Test with {{c3::bromine water}}: alkenes turn it from {{c4::orange/brown to colourless}} (decolourises). Alkanes do NOT decolourise bromine water.
What is cracking and why is it important?

Long-chain alkanes are cracked (broken down) to produce shorter, more useful molecules — alkanes and alkenes. Two methods: catalytic cracking (zeolite catalyst, ~500°C) and steam cracking (steam, very high temperature). Important because demand for short-chain hydrocarbons (fuels, plastics) exceeds supply from fractional distillation.
What are the products of complete vs incomplete combustion?

Complete combustion (excess O₂): hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. Incomplete combustion (limited O₂): produces CO (toxic, colourless, odourless) + C (soot/particulates) + H₂O. Soot causes global dimming and respiratory problems. CO is toxic — binds to haemoglobin preventing O₂ transport.
What is addition polymerisation and how do you represent the polymer from a monomer?

Many alkene monomers (with C=C double bonds) join together — the double bond opens and molecules link into a long chain. Only one product forms — atom economy = 100%.
Example: n(CH₂=CH₂) → [–CH₂–CH₂–]ₙ (polyethene/polythene)
n(CH₂=CHCl) → [–CH₂–CHCl–]ₙ (PVC)
Representing: the monomer shows C=C; the repeat unit in polymer shows single bond. Brackets and subscript n show the repeating unit.
Environmental problem: most addition polymers are non-biodegradable.
How does concentration affect the rate of a chemical reaction?
Higher concentration means more particles per unit volume — more frequent collisions between reactant particles — more successful collisions per second — faster rate of reaction.
How does temperature affect the rate of a chemical reaction?

Higher temperature gives particles more kinetic energy — they move faster — more frequent AND more energetic collisions. A greater proportion of collisions have energy equal to or greater than the activation energy — rate increases significantly. A 10°C rise roughly doubles the rate.
How does surface area affect the rate of a chemical reaction?

Increasing surface area exposes more particles of the solid reactant — more collisions per second with the other reactant — faster rate. Example: powdered marble reacts faster with HCl than marble chips of the same mass.
How does a catalyst affect the rate of a chemical reaction?

A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy — a greater proportion of collisions now have enough energy to react — faster rate. The catalyst is not used up and does not change the products or the overall energy change.
What is a catalyst and how does it work?

A substance that increases the rate of a reaction without being used up. Provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. Biological catalysts are called enzymes. Heterogeneous catalysts (different state to reactants) — e.g. iron in Haber process.

Collision theory: for a reaction to occur, particles must {{c1::collide}} with {{c2::sufficient energy}} (equal to or greater than the {{c3::activation energy}}) and with the {{c4::correct orientation}}.
What is a reversible reaction and what is dynamic equilibrium?

A reversible reaction can proceed in both directions (⇌). Dynamic equilibrium: in a closed system, the forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate — concentrations remain constant. (HT only: Le Chatelier's principle — if conditions change, the equilibrium shifts to oppose the change.)

[HT only] Le Chatelier's principle — temperature: increasing temperature shifts equilibrium in the {{c1::endothermic}} direction. Pressure: increasing pressure shifts equilibrium towards fewer {{c2::gas molecules}}. Concentration: increasing reactant concentration shifts equilibrium towards the {{c3::products}}.
What is the required practical for rate of reaction? Describe two different methods.

Method 1 — Marble chips + HCl (CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂):
• Measure mass of flask + contents on balance. CO₂ escapes → mass decreases. Record mass every 30 seconds. Plot mass vs time graph.
• OR collect CO₂ in gas syringe — plot volume vs time.
Method 2 — Sodium thiosulfate + HCl (produces sulphur precipitate — turns cloudy):
• Draw a cross on paper. Place flask over it. Add HCl. Time until cross disappears from above.
• 1/time ∝ rate. Vary: temperature, concentration of Na₂S₂O₃.
Control variables: volume of solutions, concentration of one reactant, temperature.
How do you calculate rate of reaction from a graph?

Rate = amount of product formed (or reactant used) ÷ time taken.
From a graph: rate at any point = gradient of the tangent to the curve at that point.
Initial rate = gradient at time zero (steepest part of curve — start of reaction).
Units: g/s (loss of mass method), cm³/s (gas volume method).
As reaction proceeds: gradient decreases as reactants are used up → rate decreases. Curve levels off when reaction is complete (one reactant fully used).
[HT only] What are the Haber process conditions and why are these specific conditions chosen?

Reaction: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (ΔH = −92 kJ/mol — exothermic, fewer gas molecules on right)
Conditions: temperature ~450°C, pressure ~200 atm, iron catalyst.
Temperature compromise: lower T → higher yield (Le Chatelier: exothermic, cool favours products) BUT too slow. 450°C balances reasonable yield with acceptable rate.
Pressure: higher P → higher yield (4 mol gas → 2 mol gas, high pressure favours fewer moles). 200 atm is economic limit — higher would require stronger/more expensive equipment.
Catalyst: iron catalyst increases rate without affecting equilibrium position — recycled unreacted N₂ and H₂.
How does surface area affect rate of reaction? Give an example.

Increasing surface area increases rate because there are more particles exposed at the surface — more collisions per second with the other reactant.
Example: powdered CaCO₃ reacts faster with HCl than marble chips of the same mass — same number of moles, but powder has far greater total surface area.
Practical evidence: in the marble chips/HCl required practical, using powder instead of chips → steeper gradient on mass vs time graph → faster initial rate, same total mass loss.
Relevant to explosions: fine dust (flour, coal) has enormous surface area → can explode rapidly when ignited.
How do you measure rate of reaction using the loss of mass method?

Place the flask on a balance. As a gas product escapes, record mass at regular intervals. Plot mass vs time — gradient = rate. The curve levels off when one reactant is used up. Works when the reaction produces a gas (e.g. CaCO₃ + HCl → CO₂ escapes).
How do you measure rate of reaction using the gas volume method?

Connect the flask to a gas syringe or inverted measuring cylinder over water. Record the volume of gas produced at regular intervals. Plot volume vs time — gradient = rate. Example: H₂O₂ decomposition → O₂ collected. More precise than loss of mass for small volumes.
How do you measure rate of reaction using the colour change / turbidity method?

Draw a cross on paper and place the flask over it. Add reactants and time how long until the cross disappears (obscured by a precipitate forming in the flask). Rate ∝ 1/time. Used for Na₂S₂O₃ + HCl → yellow sulphur precipitate.
Current atmosphere: approximately {{c1::78%}} nitrogen, {{c2::21%}} oxygen, {{c3::1%}} argon, {{c4::0.04%}} carbon dioxide and small amounts of other gases including water vapour.
What was the Earth's early atmosphere and how did it change?

Early atmosphere: mostly CO₂, water vapour, small amounts of methane, ammonia and nitrogen. Released by volcanic activity. As Earth cooled, water vapour condensed to form oceans. Oceans dissolved CO₂. Algae and plants evolved (photosynthesis) — CO₂ absorbed, O₂ released. Nitrogen built up as unreactive gas. CO₂ also locked in fossil fuels and limestone.

Greenhouse gases ({{c1::CO₂}}, {{c2::methane}}, {{c3::water vapour}}) absorb {{c4::long-wave (infrared) radiation}} emitted by Earth and re-radiate it back → keeps Earth warm. Increased greenhouse gases from human activity → {{c5::enhanced greenhouse effect}} → global warming.
What are the atmospheric pollutants from combustion and their effects?

CO₂: greenhouse gas → global warming. CO: toxic (binds to haemoglobin). SO₂: acid rain (dissolves in rainwater → sulfuric acid) → damages ecosystems, buildings. NO/NO₂: acid rain; formed by N₂ + O₂ at high temperatures in engines. Particulates (soot): global dimming, respiratory problems.
What is the enhanced greenhouse effect and what are its consequences?

Natural greenhouse effect: greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, H₂O vapour) absorb infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate it back → keeps Earth ~33°C warmer than without atmosphere.
Enhanced greenhouse effect: increased burning of fossil fuels + deforestation → higher CO₂ levels + methane from agriculture/landfill → more heat trapped → global warming.
Consequences: rising sea levels (thermal expansion + ice melt), more extreme weather events, loss of habitats, species extinction, disruption to agriculture/water supplies, ocean acidification.
How did the proportion of oxygen in the atmosphere increase over billions of years?

Early atmosphere: no oxygen — mainly CO₂, water vapour, small amounts of N₂, methane, ammonia.
Stage 1 (~3.5 billion years ago): oceans formed as water vapour condensed. CO₂ dissolved in oceans (forming carbonate rocks and limestone over time).
Stage 2: simple photosynthetic organisms (blue-green algae/cyanobacteria) evolved → produced O₂ by photosynthesis: CO₂ + H₂O → glucose + O₂.
Stage 3: O₂ accumulated over hundreds of millions of years as organisms spread. O₂ allowed ozone layer to form → shielded from UV → more complex life could colonise land. N₂ built up as unreactive byproduct of processes.

Carbon dioxide in the atmosphere: increased by {{c1::burning fossil fuels}}, {{c2::deforestation}} (less photosynthesis, decomposition of trees), {{c3::cement production}} (CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂). Decreased by {{c4::photosynthesis}}, dissolution in {{c5::oceans}}, formation of {{c6::sedimentary rocks and fossil fuels}}.
What is the difference between potable water and pure water?

Pure water contains only H₂O molecules. Potable water is safe to drink but may contain dissolved minerals — it is not 100% pure. Treatment of fresh water: screening → sedimentation → filtration → chlorination. Desalination (for seawater): distillation or reverse osmosis — both energy-intensive.
What are the four stages of sewage treatment?

1. Screening — removes large solids. 2. Primary treatment — sedimentation, sludge settles. 3. Secondary treatment — aeration tank, aerobic bacteria break down organic matter. 4. Sludge digestion — anaerobic bacteria produce biogas; dried sludge used as fertiliser.
What is the life cycle assessment (LCA)?

Evaluates the environmental impact of a product across its entire life: raw material extraction, manufacturing, use, and disposal. Considers energy use, water use, waste production and pollution at each stage. Used to compare products and make more sustainable choices. Can be biased depending on assumptions made.

Finite resources: {{c1::fossil fuels}}, metal ores — cannot be replaced on a human timescale. Renewable resources: {{c2::wood}}, {{c3::plant-based materials}} — can be replaced. Recycling metals saves {{c4::energy}} and reduces need to mine new ore (reduces habitat destruction).
What are the environmental advantages of recycling metals compared to extracting from ores?

Advantages of recycling:
• Much less energy required — e.g. recycling aluminium uses only ~5% of the energy needed for extraction from bauxite by electrolysis.
• Reduces mining → less habitat destruction, less toxic waste from mining operations.
• Reduces CO₂ emissions (less fossil fuel burned for energy).
• Conserves finite ore reserves.
• Reduces landfill waste.
Challenges: sorting different metals is time-consuming and expensive. Some alloys are difficult to separate. Some contamination from other materials.
What is the Haber process for making ammonia and how is it related to using resources sustainably?

N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (450°C, 200 atm, iron catalyst)
Sustainability issues: nitrogen comes from air (unlimited). Hydrogen comes from methane (natural gas) — finite resource that produces CO₂.
Alternative: electrolysis of water using renewable electricity to produce hydrogen — but currently expensive.
Ammonia use: mainly for making nitrogen fertilisers (e.g. ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulfate) → increases crop yields → supports global population. However, excess fertiliser → eutrophication of water.
What is radioactive decay and what are the three types?

Unstable nuclei emit radiation spontaneously to become more stable. Alpha (α): 2 protons + 2 neutrons (helium nucleus) — most ionising, least penetrating (stopped by paper/skin). Beta (β): high-speed electron from nucleus — moderately ionising (stopped by aluminium). Gamma (γ): electromagnetic radiation — least ionising, most penetrating (reduced by thick lead/concrete).

Alpha decay: atomic number decreases by {{c1::2}}, mass number decreases by {{c2::4}}. Beta decay: atomic number increases by {{c3::1}}, mass number stays the {{c4::same}} (a neutron becomes a proton + electron).

Half-life: time taken for {{c1::half}} the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay. After 2 half-lives: {{c2::25%}} remains. After 3 half-lives: {{c3::12.5%}} remains. Used for {{c4::radioactive dating}} of rocks and organic material.
What are the uses of nuclear radiation?

Medical: gamma used in radiotherapy (kills cancer cells), beta used in tracers. Industrial: gamma used to check for cracks in pipes/metal. Carbon-14 dating (archaeological). Nuclear power: fission of uranium-235 releases energy. Nuclear fusion (stars, potential future energy): joins nuclei together, releases enormous energy.
What is nuclear fission and how does a chain reaction occur?

Nuclear fission: a large, unstable nucleus (e.g. uranium-235 or plutonium-239) absorbs a slow-moving neutron → splits into two smaller nuclei + 2–3 neutrons + large amount of energy (kinetic energy of fragments + gamma radiation).
Chain reaction: the neutrons released go on to cause further fissions → exponentially increasing reactions. In a nuclear reactor, control rods (boron) absorb excess neutrons to control the rate. The moderator (water/graphite) slows neutrons so they can be absorbed by nuclei.
What is nuclear fusion and why is it difficult to achieve on Earth?

Nuclear fusion: two small nuclei (e.g. hydrogen isotopes — deuterium and tritium) combine under extreme conditions to form a larger nucleus, releasing enormous energy.
Why difficult: nuclei are positively charged → repel each other → need extremely high temperatures (millions of °C) and high pressure to force nuclei close enough to fuse. Difficult to contain such a plasma. Current fusion reactors (e.g. JET) require more energy input than they produce.
Advantages if achieved: huge energy release, abundant fuel (hydrogen from seawater), no long-lived radioactive waste, no CO₂.

Background radiation: always-present low-level radiation from natural and artificial sources. Natural sources: {{c1::radon gas}} (from rocks — major source in UK), {{c2::cosmic rays}}, {{c3::food and drink}}, {{c4::rocks and building materials}}. Artificial: {{c5::medical (X-rays/nuclear medicine)}}, nuclear power, nuclear weapons testing. Background radiation must be {{c6::subtracted}} from measurements in experiments.
What is the difference between contamination and irradiation?

Irradiation: the body is exposed to radiation from a source outside the body. The source is not in contact — once removed, exposure stops. Less dangerous than contamination (depending on type).
Contamination: radioactive material gets onto or inside the body (inhaled, ingested, skin contact). Continues to irradiate from inside — much harder to remove. Alpha emitters are especially dangerous as contaminants (very ionising internally) even though alpha is stopped by skin externally.
Worked example: half-life calculation. A sample initially contains 6400 atoms of a radioactive isotope with half-life 3 years. How many atoms remain after 12 years?

Number of half-lives = 12 ÷ 3 = 4 half-lives
After 1 half-life (3 yr): 6400 ÷ 2 = 3200
After 2 half-lives (6 yr): 3200 ÷ 2 = 1600
After 3 half-lives (9 yr): 1600 ÷ 2 = 800
After 4 half-lives (12 yr): 800 ÷ 2 = 400 atoms
Formula shortcut: N = N₀ × (½)ⁿ where n = number of half-lives. N = 6400 × (½)⁴ = 6400/16 = 400.
How do you balance nuclear equations (alpha and beta decay)?

Rules: mass numbers (top) must balance. Atomic numbers (bottom) must balance.
Alpha decay: α particle = ⁴₂He. Mass number −4, atomic number −2.
Example: ²³⁸₉₂U → ²³⁴₉₀Th + ⁴₂He ✓ (238=234+4; 92=90+2)
Beta decay: β particle = ⁰₋₁e. Mass number unchanged, atomic number +1.
Example: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e ✓ (14=14+0; 6=7+(−1))
Key: gamma (γ) emission — no change to mass or atomic number (just energy released).
What are the dangers of ionising radiation and how do we protect against them?

Dangers: ionising radiation removes electrons from atoms → damages cells → kills cells (high doses) or causes mutations → cancer. Alpha most ionising (most dangerous if contaminating), gamma most penetrating (requires heavy shielding).
Reducing exposure — ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable):
• Time: minimise time in radiation field
• Distance: stay as far away as possible
• Shielding: paper stops α; aluminium stops β; thick lead/concrete stops γ
• Wear dosimeters to monitor accumulated dose. Use tongs, not hands.
Charge (Q) = {{c1::current (I)}} × {{c2::time (t)}}. Q in {{c3::coulombs (C)}}, I in {{c4::amperes (A)}}, t in {{c5::seconds (s)}}. 3A flowing for 10s: Q = {{c6::30C}}.
Ohm's Law: {{c1::V = IR}}. V = voltage/potential difference ({{c2::volts, V}}), I = current ({{c3::amps, A}}), R = resistance ({{c4::ohms, Ω}}). A 12V battery with 3Ω resistance: I = {{c5::4A}}.
What is the difference between series and parallel circuits?

Series: components connected in one loop. Same current through all. Total resistance = sum of individual resistances. Voltage shared. One breaks — all break. Parallel: components connected in separate branches. Same voltage across each branch. Total resistance less than smallest individual resistance. One breaks — others work.
Power equations: P = {{c1::IV}} = {{c2::I²R}} = {{c3::V²/R}}. Units: {{c4::watts (W)}}. A 230V device drawing 2A: P = {{c5::460W}}.
What are the I-V characteristics of a resistor, filament lamp and diode?

Resistor: straight line through origin (constant resistance — Ohm's law). Filament lamp: curved — as current increases, temperature increases, resistance increases. Diode: only allows current in one direction — very high resistance in reverse direction.
A thermistor's resistance {{c1::decreases}} as temperature increases. An LDR's resistance {{c2::decreases}} as light intensity increases. Both used in {{c3::sensing circuits}}.
What is the National Grid and why are transformers used?

The National Grid transmits electricity from power stations to homes and industry. Step-up transformers increase voltage (and decrease current) for transmission — reduces energy loss due to resistance in cables. Step-down transformers reduce voltage to safe levels for use (230V in homes).

Mains electricity in the UK: {{c1::230V}} AC at {{c2::50Hz}}. AC = {{c3::alternating current}} (direction changes). DC = {{c4::direct current}} (one direction, from batteries). Live wire: {{c5::brown}}. Neutral: {{c6::blue}}. Earth: {{c7::green and yellow}} (safety).
How do you calculate resistance in a series circuit?

Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃... (resistances add up). Current same throughout. Voltage shared proportionally (larger R → larger V across it).
Example: two 6Ω resistors in series: R_total = 12Ω.
Parallel (qualitative): total resistance is less than the smallest individual resistor. Same voltage across each branch. Currents add up (I_total = I₁ + I₂). Note: calculating total resistance in parallel is not required.
What is the required practical for I-V characteristics? What graphs do you draw?

Method: set up circuit with ammeter in series, voltmeter in parallel with the component. Use variable resistor (rheostat) to change current. Record current and voltage for range of values. Reverse connections to get negative values.
Components tested:
• Resistor: straight line through origin → constant resistance (Ohm's Law).
• Filament lamp: S-curve (non-linear) → resistance increases as temperature increases.
• Diode: current only flows in one direction. Very high resistance in reverse direction → flat line.
How do we stay safe from mains electricity? What is the role of the earth wire and fuse?

Earth wire (green/yellow): connects metal casing of appliance to earth (0V). If live wire touches casing → large current flows through earth wire → fuse blows → appliance isolated. Prevents electrocution.
Fuse: thin wire that melts if too much current flows → breaks the circuit. Chosen to be slightly above normal operating current. E.g. 2A device → 3A fuse (NOT 5A — too slow to protect).
Circuit breaker: automatically switches off if current exceeds threshold. Can be reset (unlike fuse which must be replaced). RCDs (residual current device) detect very small differences in current → trip in milliseconds.

The eight energy stores: {{c1::kinetic}}, {{c2::gravitational potential}}, {{c3::elastic potential}}, {{c4::chemical}}, {{c5::thermal}}, {{c6::magnetic}}, {{c7::electrostatic}}, {{c8::nuclear}}. Energy is conserved — it is {{c9::transferred}} between stores, never created or destroyed.

Energy is transferred by four pathways: {{c1::mechanically}} (by forces doing work), {{c2::electrically}} (by electric current), {{c3::by heating}} (from hot to cold), {{c4::by radiation}} (e.g. light, infrared, sound waves).
Kinetic energy: Ek = {{c1::½mv²}}. Where m = {{c2::mass (kg)}}, v = {{c3::speed (m/s)}}. E.g. 2kg object at 3m/s: Ek = 0.5 × 2 × 9 = {{c4::9 J}}.

Gravitational potential energy: Ep = {{c1::mgh}}. Where m = mass (kg), g = {{c2::9.8 m/s²}} (gravitational field strength), h = {{c3::height (m)}}. E.g. 5kg object raised 10m: Ep = 5 × 9.8 × 10 = {{c4::490 J}}.
Power = {{c1::energy transferred}} ÷ {{c2::time}}. Unit: {{c3::watts (W)}}. 1W = 1 J/s. A device with 60J in 3s: power = {{c4::20W}}.
What is the difference between renewable and non-renewable energy resources?

Non-renewable: fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) + nuclear — will run out, provide reliable baseload power, produce CO₂ (except nuclear). Renewable: solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal, tidal, wave, biofuel — won't run out, often intermittent, smaller environmental footprint.

Efficiency = {{c1::useful output energy}} ÷ {{c2::total input energy}} (× 100 for %). A device that transfers 80J usefully from 200J input: efficiency = {{c3::80/200 = 0.4 or 40%}}. Wasted energy goes to {{c4::thermal energy}} (heat).
How is energy transferred in heating and what reduces unwanted transfer?

Conduction: vibrations passed between particles (solids). Convection: hot fluid rises, cool sinks — creates a current (fluids). Radiation: infrared electromagnetic waves — no medium needed. Reducing: insulation (traps air), double glazing, cavity walls, draught excluders, reflective foil.
What is a Sankey diagram and how do you interpret one?

A Sankey diagram represents energy transfers. The arrow width is proportional to the amount of energy.
Reading one: input energy enters from the left (total width). Useful output goes straight ahead. Wasted energy (usually heat) branches off — often downwards.
Example: a filament bulb with 100J input: 10J useful light (forward arrow), 90J waste heat (downward arrow).
Efficiency from Sankey: useful output ÷ total input × 100 = 10/100 × 100 = 10% efficient.
Exam tip: the wasted energy arrow should be wider than the useful arrow for most devices (most real processes are <50% efficient).
What are the advantages and disadvantages of fossil fuels as an energy resource?

Advantages: reliable baseload power, high energy density, existing infrastructure worldwide.
Disadvantages: finite (non-renewable), burning releases CO₂ (climate change) and SO₂ (acid rain), coal mining damages landscapes.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of nuclear power?

Advantages: very reliable, no CO₂ during generation, very high energy density (small fuel needed).
Disadvantages: radioactive waste (difficult to dispose of safely), very expensive to build and decommission, risk of accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima).
What are the advantages and disadvantages of wind energy?

Advantages: renewable, no CO₂ during operation, cheap to run once built.
Disadvantages: intermittent (only works when wind blows), visual and noise impact, potential harm to birds. Offshore is more reliable but more expensive.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of solar energy?

Advantages: renewable, no CO₂ or pollution during operation, no moving parts (low maintenance).
Disadvantages: intermittent (only works in daylight/clear weather), large land area required for solar farms, manufacturing panels has environmental cost.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of hydroelectric power?

Advantages: reliable and renewable, no CO₂ during operation, can store energy (pump water up at off-peak times).
Disadvantages: requires flooding valleys — destroys habitats and displaces communities. Limited to suitable geography.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of biofuels?

Advantages: renewable, theoretically carbon-neutral (CO₂ released equals CO₂ absorbed while growing).
Disadvantages: land used for fuel crops competes with food production. Growing and processing biofuels still has significant carbon footprint in practice.

Elastic potential energy: stored in stretched or compressed springs. Formula: Ep = {{c1::½ke²}}. k = {{c2::spring constant}} (N/m), e = {{c3::extension}} (m). A spring with k = 50 N/m stretched by 0.2 m: Ep = 0.5 × 50 × 0.04 = {{c4::1 J}}.
What are the properties of solids, liquids and gases in terms of particle theory?

Solid: particles close together in fixed positions, vibrate — fixed shape and volume. Liquid: particles close together but can move — fixed volume, no fixed shape. Gas: particles far apart, move randomly and fast — no fixed shape or volume. As energy increases, particles move faster.

Specific heat capacity (SHC): energy needed to raise the temperature of {{c1::1kg}} of a substance by {{c2::1°C}}. Formula: Q = {{c3::mcΔT}}. Water has a high SHC of {{c4::4200 J/kg°C}} — ideal for central heating.

Specific latent heat: energy required to change the state of {{c1::1kg}} of a substance without changing {{c2::temperature}}. Fusion (melting): Q = {{c3::mL_f}}. Vaporisation (boiling): Q = {{c4::mL_v}}. During state changes, energy breaks/forms intermolecular bonds — temperature stays {{c5::constant}}.
What is gas pressure and how does it change with temperature?

Gas pressure caused by particles colliding with walls of container. Increasing temperature → particles move faster → more frequent and forceful collisions → increased pressure (if volume constant). Increasing volume → fewer collisions per unit area → decreased pressure (if temperature constant).

Density = {{c1::mass}} ÷ {{c2::volume}}. Units: {{c3::kg/m³}} or {{c4::g/cm³}}. A block of mass 500g and volume 250 cm³ has density = {{c5::2 g/cm³}}. Density of water = {{c6::1 g/cm³}} (1000 kg/m³). Objects less dense than water {{c7::float}}.
What is the required practical for measuring density? How do you find the volume of irregular objects?

Regular objects: measure dimensions with ruler/vernier callipers → calculate volume (e.g. l×w×h for cuboid). Measure mass on digital balance → density = mass/volume.
Irregular objects (e.g. stone): partially fill a measuring cylinder with water, note the volume. Submerge the object → note new volume. Volume of object = difference in water levels (displacement method).
Measure mass on balance → density = mass/volume.
Units: if mass in g and volume in cm³ → density in g/cm³.
Worked example: SHC calculation. A 2 kg block of copper (SHC = 385 J/kg°C) is heated and its temperature rises from 20°C to 70°C. How much energy was transferred?

Q = mcΔT
m = 2 kg, c = 385 J/kg°C, ΔT = 70 − 20 = 50°C
Q = 2 × 385 × 50 = 38,500 J = 38.5 kJ
Rearranging: c = Q/(mΔT) to find SHC. m = Q/(cΔT) to find mass. ΔT = Q/(mc) to find temperature change.
What is the required practical for specific heat capacity? What is the method?

Equipment: metal block (aluminium or copper) with two holes, immersion heater, thermometer, joulemeter or ammeter+voltmeter+stopwatch, insulating material.
Method: insert heater and thermometer into block. Record starting temperature. Switch on heater, record temperature every minute and the total energy supplied (from joulemeter: E = IVt).
Calculate: c = Q ÷ (mΔT) where Q = energy from joulemeter, m = mass of block, ΔT = temperature rise.
Wrap block in insulating material to reduce energy lost to surroundings → more accurate result.
Worked example: specific latent heat. How much energy is needed to melt 0.5 kg of ice? (Latent heat of fusion of water = 334,000 J/kg)

Q = mL
Q = 0.5 × 334,000 = 167,000 J = 167 kJ
Key point: during melting, temperature stays at 0°C — all energy breaks intermolecular bonds, none goes into kinetic energy of particles.
Latent heat of vaporisation (boiling water → steam) = 2,260,000 J/kg — much larger because bonds between molecules must be completely separated.

(Higher Tier) Boyle's Law: for a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, pressure × volume = {{c1::constant}}. Formula: {{c2::p₁V₁ = p₂V₂}}. If pressure doubles, volume {{c3::halves}} (inversely proportional). Example: gas at 100 kPa occupies 2 m³. If pressure increases to 200 kPa: new volume = {{c4::1 m³}}.
(Higher Tier) What is the pressure-temperature law for gases and how do you use it?

At constant volume: p/T = constant, so p₁/T₁ = p₂/T₂.
Important: temperature MUST be in Kelvin (K). T(K) = T(°C) + 273.
Example: gas at 100 kPa and 27°C (= 300 K). Heated to 127°C (= 400 K) at constant volume.
p₂ = p₁ × T₂/T₁ = 100 × 400/300 = 133 kPa
Explanation: higher temperature → faster particles → more frequent/harder collisions with walls → greater pressure.
What is Brownian motion and what does it show about particles?

Brownian motion: the random, jittery movement of small particles (e.g. smoke or pollen) suspended in a fluid, observed under a microscope.
Explanation: the particles are constantly being bombarded by much smaller, invisible molecules of the fluid moving randomly. The bombardment is uneven — more molecules hit from one side at any moment → net force → random movement.
Significance: provides evidence that fluids are made of tiny, constantly moving particles (kinetic theory). First explained by Einstein in 1905.
What is the difference between scalar and vector quantities?

Scalar: magnitude only (e.g. speed, distance, mass, temperature, time). Vector: magnitude AND direction (e.g. velocity, displacement, acceleration, force, momentum, weight). Vectors are represented by arrows.

Speed = {{c1::distance}} Ã· {{c2::time}}. Velocity = {{c3::displacement}} Ã· time. Acceleration = {{c4::change in velocity}} Ã· {{c5::time}}. Units: speed/velocity = {{c6::m/s}}, acceleration = {{c7::m/sÂ²}}.
What do distance-time and velocity-time graphs show?

Distance-time: gradient = speed. Horizontal line = stationary. Curved line = acceleration. Velocity-time: gradient = acceleration. Area under graph = distance. Horizontal line = constant velocity. Negative gradient = deceleration.
Newton's Second Law: {{c1::F = ma}}. Force in {{c2::Newtons (N)}}, mass in {{c3::kg}}, acceleration in {{c4::m/sÂ²}}. A 1000kg car accelerating at 2m/sÂ²: F = {{c5::2000N}}.
What are the three laws of motion?

1st: An object remains at rest or moves with constant velocity unless acted on by a resultant force. 2nd: F = ma (resultant force = mass Ã— acceleration). 3rd: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction â€” forces act on different objects.

Stopping distance = {{c1::thinking distance}} + {{c2::braking distance}}. Thinking distance affected by: {{c3::speed, tiredness, alcohol, drugs}}. Braking distance affected by: {{c4::speed, road conditions, tyre condition, brake condition}}. Braking force causes {{c5::deceleration}}.

Weight = {{c1::mass}} Ã— {{c2::gravitational field strength (g)}}. On Earth, g = {{c3::9.8 N/kg}}. A 50kg person: weight = {{c4::490 N}}. Weight is a {{c5::vector}} (has direction â€” downwards). Mass is a {{c6::scalar}}.

[HT only] Momentum = {{c1::mass}} Ã— {{c2::velocity}}. Units: {{c3::kg m/s}}. Law of conservation of momentum: total momentum before = total momentum after in a {{c4::closed system}} (no external forces). Force = {{c5::change in momentum}} Ã· time.
What is Hooke's Law and what is the spring constant?

Hooke's Law: extension of a spring is directly proportional to the force applied, up to the limit of proportionality (elastic limit).
Formula: F = ke (Force = spring constant Ã— extension). k in N/m, e in m.
Graph: linear section (Hooke's Law applies) then curves upward past the elastic limit (plastic deformation â€” doesn't return to original shape).
Worked example: 5N stretches a spring by 0.1m â†’ k = F/e = 5/0.1 = 50 N/m. Predict extension for 7.5N: e = F/k = 7.5/50 = 0.15 m.
Required practical: measure extension for increasing weights, plot F vs extension.
What are terminal velocity and the forces on falling objects?

Falling object: initially, weight > drag â†’ accelerates. As speed increases, drag increases. Eventually drag = weight â†’ resultant force = 0 â†’ constant velocity = terminal velocity.
Graph (velocity vs time): curve increasing, then horizontal line at terminal velocity.
Parachute example: when parachute opens â†’ drag suddenly increases greatly â†’ deceleration. New, lower terminal velocity reached. On landing, speed is safe.
Factors affecting terminal velocity: weight (greater weight â†’ higher terminal velocity), drag (larger area/higher drag coefficient â†’ lower terminal velocity).
What is the difference between permanent and induced magnets?

Permanent magnets: always magnetic (e.g. bar magnet — made of steel). Induced magnets: temporarily magnetic when in a magnetic field (made of iron). Magnetic field lines run from north to south pole, closer together = stronger field.
[HT only] What is the motor effect and what factors affect the force?

A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force (the motor effect). F = BIL (force = magnetic flux density × current × length). Direction given by Fleming's Left Hand Rule: thuMb = Motion, First finger = Field, seCond finger = Current.
What is electromagnetic induction?

Moving a conductor through a magnetic field (or changing the magnetic field around a conductor) induces a potential difference — and a current if the circuit is complete. The generator effect. Increasing the speed, field strength, or number of turns increases the induced EMF.

A transformer uses {{c1::electromagnetic induction}}. Step-up transformer: {{c2::more}} turns on secondary coil → higher voltage. Step-down: {{c3::fewer}} turns → lower voltage. Formula: {{c4::Vs/Vp = Ns/Np}}. Only works with {{c5::AC}} (alternating current).

[HT only] Transformer worked example: a step-up transformer has 500 turns on the primary and 5000 turns on the secondary. If the primary voltage is 230V, what is the secondary voltage? If the primary current is 10A, what is the secondary current (assume 100% efficiency)?

Voltage: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np → Vs = 230 × (5000/500) = 2300 V
Current (using P = IV, assume 100% efficient → power in = power out):
Ip × Vp = Is × Vs → 10 × 230 = Is × 2300 → Is = 1 A
Key principle: step-up voltage = step-down current. Transformers conserve power (assuming no losses).
What is a solenoid (electromagnet) and what factors affect its strength?

A solenoid is a coil of wire that acts as a magnet when current flows through it. The magnetic field inside is uniform and similar to a bar magnet (with N and S poles).
Factors that increase strength: increase the current, increase the number of turns per unit length, add a soft iron core (greatly amplifies the field).
Advantage over permanent magnets: can be switched on and off, and strength can be varied.
Uses: electric bells, circuit breakers, relays, MRI scanners.
[HT only] What is the structure of a DC motor and how does it work?

A DC motor converts electrical energy into kinetic (rotational) energy using the motor effect.
Components: rectangular coil of wire in a magnetic field, commutator (split-ring — reverses current direction every half turn), carbon brushes (conduct current to the commutator).
How it works: current in coil → force (motor effect) → coil rotates. Commutator ensures current always reverses so the coil continues to rotate in the same direction rather than oscillating.
Increase speed: more current, stronger magnetic field, more turns on coil.
Why does the National Grid transmit electricity at high voltage? How does this reduce energy loss?

Transmitting at high voltage means the current is low (P = IV, power is constant).
Energy lost in cables = I²R — power loss depends on the square of the current.
Halving the current → power loss reduced by a factor of 4.
Process: power station generates ~25,000V → step-up transformer → 400,000V for transmission (very low current) → step-down transformers reduce to 230V for homes.
Exam tip: it's the LOW CURRENT that reduces energy loss, not the high voltage directly. High voltage enables low current.

[HT only] Fleming's Left Hand Rule (motor effect): thuMb = {{c1::Motion/force}} (direction of movement), First finger = {{c2::magnetic Field}}, seCond finger = {{c3::Current}}. Used when a current-carrying conductor is in a magnetic field.
Wave equation: {{c1::v = fλ}}. v = wave speed ({{c2::m/s}}), f = frequency ({{c3::Hz}}), λ = wavelength ({{c4::m}}). A wave with frequency 200Hz and wavelength 2m: speed = {{c5::400 m/s}}.
What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves?

Transverse: oscillations perpendicular to direction of travel (e.g. light, water waves, electromagnetic waves). Longitudinal: oscillations parallel to direction of travel (e.g. sound, P-waves). Longitudinal waves have compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure).
What are the electromagnetic spectrum and its uses?

Radio waves: TV/radio communication. Microwaves: satellite communication, cooking. Infrared: thermal imaging, TV remotes, optical fibres. Visible light: sight, photography. UV: fluorescent lamps, sterilisation, detecting forged bank notes. X-rays: medical imaging, security scanning. Gamma: cancer treatment, sterilising medical equipment. All travel at 3×10⁸ m/s in a vacuum.

Refraction: waves change {{c1::speed}} (and direction if hitting at an angle) when passing between media. Light slows down in {{c2::denser}} materials — bends {{c3::towards the normal}}. Used in {{c4::lenses}} and optical fibres.
How are sound waves produced and what affects their properties?

Sound: longitudinal wave produced by vibrating objects. Travels through solids, liquids and gases (not vacuum). Frequency determines pitch (higher f = higher pitch). Amplitude determines loudness. Speed in air ~330 m/s. Ultrasound (>20,000 Hz) used in medical imaging and sonar.

The EM spectrum in order of increasing frequency (lowest to highest): {{c1::radio waves}}, {{c2::microwaves}}, {{c3::infrared}}, {{c4::visible light}}, {{c5::ultraviolet}}, {{c6::X-rays}}, {{c7::gamma rays}}. All travel at {{c8::3×10⁸ m/s}} in a vacuum.
Going from radio to gamma: frequency {{c1::increases}}, wavelength {{c2::decreases}}, energy {{c3::increases}}. Higher frequency = more {{c4::ionising}} and more {{c5::dangerous}} to living tissue.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of radio waves?

Longest wavelength, lowest frequency and energy. Produced by oscillating electrons. Uses: TV and radio broadcasting, Bluetooth, WiFi. Hazards: generally considered safe at normal exposure levels — non-ionising.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of microwaves?

Between radio and infrared. Absorbed by water molecules — causes heating. Uses: cooking (microwave ovens), satellite communication, mobile phones, radar. Hazards: can heat body tissue internally — non-ionising but can cause heating of cells.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of infrared radiation?

Just below visible light. All objects emit infrared — hotter objects emit more. Detected by skin as heat. Uses: thermal imaging cameras, TV remotes, optical fibre communication, night vision, toasters/grills, physiotherapy. Hazards: can cause skin burns and eye damage at high intensities.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of visible light?

The only part of the EM spectrum detectable by the human eye. Range from red (lowest frequency) to violet (highest). Uses: sight, photography, optical fibres for communication, lasers (surgery, barcode scanners). Hazards: high intensity lasers or bright light can damage the retina.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of ultraviolet (UV) radiation?

Higher frequency than visible light — ionising. Produced by the Sun and UV lamps. Uses: detecting forged bank notes (security markings fluoresce), sterilising water/medical equipment, tanning beds. Hazards: damages DNA → skin cancer, damages eyes → cataracts. Ozone layer absorbs most UV from the Sun.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of X-rays?

High frequency, short wavelength — ionising. Produced by firing electrons at metal targets. Absorbed by dense materials (bone/metal), pass through soft tissue. Uses: medical imaging (bone fractures, dental checks), security scanning (airports), checking welds in industry. Hazards: ionises cells → can cause mutations and cancer. Radiographers move away or use lead screens.
What are the properties, uses and hazards of gamma rays?

Highest frequency, shortest wavelength — most ionising. Emitted by unstable atomic nuclei (radioactive decay). Uses: cancer treatment (radiotherapy — kills tumour cells), sterilising medical equipment, food irradiation (kills bacteria), PET scans. Hazards: highly ionising — damages/kills cells and DNA → cancer. Reduced by thick lead or concrete.

Non-ionising radiation ({{c1::radio, microwave, infrared, visible}}) does not have enough energy to remove electrons from atoms. Ionising radiation ({{c2::UV, X-rays, gamma}}) has enough energy to ionise atoms, potentially causing {{c3::cancer}} by damaging {{c4::DNA}}.
What is the difference between reflection, absorption and transmission of EM waves?

Reflection: wave bounces off a surface (angle of incidence = angle of reflection). Absorption: wave transfers energy to the material — causes heating. Transmission: wave passes through the material. Different materials reflect, absorb and transmit different parts of the EM spectrum — e.g. glass transmits visible light but absorbs UV.

Amplitude = {{c1::maximum displacement}} from rest position. Frequency = number of {{c2::complete waves per second}} (Hz). Wavelength = distance from one {{c3::crest to the next crest}} (or trough to trough). Period = {{c4::1 ÷ frequency}} (time for one complete wave).
What is the required practical for waves? How do you measure the speed of waves?

Ripple tank (water waves): measure wavelength (distance between wave crests on stroboscope or freeze-frame). Count frequency (oscillations per second). Speed = fλ.
Sound waves in a solid (slinky): send a wave pulse, measure time for it to travel known distance. Speed = distance/time.
Measuring speed of sound in air: stand far from a wall. Clap boards together and time the echo (echo = sound travels twice the distance). Speed ≈ 330 m/s. Or use two microphones connected to an oscilloscope — measure time delay for sound to travel between them.
